item.title: Latitudinal manganese gradient dynamics associated with Earth’s major ice ages
item.dc:creator: Xubin Wang
Alexandre Pohl
Rosalind E. M. Rickaby
Zunli Lu
item.description: Greater sedimentary manganese enrichments are found at relatively higher latitudes during Earth’s six major glaciations. This manganese gradient coevolved with atmospheric oxygen level and oceanic oxygen gradients. Distinct sedimentary deposits of manganese are known to be associated with extreme glaciations in ancient environments. The redox-sensitive nature of manganese led scientists to propose theories about how oxygen and climate collaborated to cause major perturbations in the global geochemical cycle of this important metal. Temporal analyses of a large sedimentary dataset demonstrate high manganese concentrations in marine sediments were commonly associated with oxygenation events and glaciations in deep time. Here we show spatial analyses of those intervals that reveal a common pattern of relative enrichment of manganese in sediments at high latitudes during the six greatest glaciations of Earth’s history. The pattern of relatively lower manganese concentrations in tropical sediments was most likely driven by ocean oxygen gradients. This manganese gradient increasingly flattened as the oxygen level rose in the atmosphere and ocean over the geological time scale. Our analyses suggest that anoxia frequently occurred during icehouse conditions, challenging the conventional wisdom of warming-driven ocean deoxygenation. Published: 2026-07-13T16:33:21+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.536 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xubin Wang; Alexandre Pohl; Rosalind E. M. Rickaby; Zunli Lu

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-75597-2

Relevance: 0.536 (flagship)

Greater sedimentary manganese enrichments are found at relatively higher latitudes during Earth’s six major glaciations. This manganese gradient coevolved with atmospheric oxygen level and oceanic oxygen gradients. Distinct sedimentary deposits of manganese are known to be associated with extreme glaciations in ancient environments. The redox-sensitive nature of manganese led scientists to propose theories about how oxygen and climate collaborated to cause major perturbations in the global geochemical cycle of this important metal. Temporal analyses of a large sedimentary dataset demonstrate high manganese concentrations in marine sediments were commonly associated with oxygenation events and glaciations in deep time. Here we show spatial analyses of those intervals that reveal a common pattern of relative enrichment of manganese in sediments at high latitudes during the six greatest glaciations of Earth’s history. The pattern of relatively lower manganese concentrations in tropical sediments was most likely driven by ocean oxygen gradients. This manganese gradient increasingly flattened as the oxygen level rose in the atmosphere and ocean over the geological time scale. Our analyses suggest that anoxia frequently occurred during icehouse conditions, challenging the conventional wisdom of warming-driven ocean deoxygenation.

item.title: Organic–inorganic carbon coupling shapes carbon dioxide fluxes in seagrass ecosystems
item.dc:creator: Mariche B. Natividad
Wei-Jun Cai
Nina Bednaršek
Jian-Jhih Chen
Wen-Chen Chou
item.description: Seagrass meadows can remove atmospheric carbon dioxide through organic carbon production and storage, but they also alter seawater chemistry through calcium carbonate formation and dissolution. Most blue carbon assessments focus on organic carbon burial and overlook how inorganic carbon cycling regulates carbon dioxide exchange. This Perspective presents a framework linking organic carbon metabolism, carbonate cycling, and sedimentary processes to explain when seagrass ecosystems function as net carbon dioxide sinks or sources. We introduce a compensation ratio that defines the balance between organic carbon uptake and calcification-driven carbon dioxide release and show how this balance changes with environmental conditions and sediment carbon sources. Using the Dongsha Island lagoon as a case study, we show how seagrass metabolism coupled to dissolution of geologically derived carbonate sediments can sustain alkalinity generation and long-term carbon dioxide uptake. Together, this framework provides a more complete basis for evaluating blue carbon and the climate mitigation potential of carbonate-rich coastal ecosystems. By linking organic metabolism, carbonate cycling, and sedimentary processes, this paper provides a comprehensive framework to accurately evaluate the climate mitigation potential of seagrass ecosystems. Case study data from the Dongsha Island lagoon reveal how coupled benthic processes can drive sustained, year-round atmospheric CO_2 uptake. Published: 2026-07-13T12:07:19+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.553 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mariche B. Natividad; Wei-Jun Cai; Nina Bednaršek; Jian-Jhih Chen; Wen-Chen Chou

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03805-4

Relevance: 0.553 (flagship)

Seagrass meadows can remove atmospheric carbon dioxide through organic carbon production and storage, but they also alter seawater chemistry through calcium carbonate formation and dissolution. Most blue carbon assessments focus on organic carbon burial and overlook how inorganic carbon cycling regulates carbon dioxide exchange. This Perspective presents a framework linking organic carbon metabolism, carbonate cycling, and sedimentary processes to explain when seagrass ecosystems function as net carbon dioxide sinks or sources. We introduce a compensation ratio that defines the balance between organic carbon uptake and calcification-driven carbon dioxide release and show how this balance changes with environmental conditions and sediment carbon sources. Using the Dongsha Island lagoon as a case study, we show how seagrass metabolism coupled to dissolution of geologically derived carbonate sediments can sustain alkalinity generation and long-term carbon dioxide uptake. Together, this framework provides a more complete basis for evaluating blue carbon and the climate mitigation potential of carbonate-rich coastal ecosystems. By linking organic metabolism, carbonate cycling, and sedimentary processes, this paper provides a comprehensive framework to accurately evaluate the climate mitigation potential of seagrass ecosystems. Case study data from the Dongsha Island lagoon reveal how coupled benthic processes can drive sustained, year-round atmospheric CO_2 uptake.

item.title: Dominant drivers and projected increases in soil cracking risk across China
item.dc:creator: Ting Wang
Chao-Sheng Tang
Zhixiong Zeng
Farshid Vahedifard
Qing Cheng
Ben-Gang Tian
Jin-Jian Xu
Zhengtao Shen
Bin Shi
item.description: Soil plasticity and water content drive elevated soil cracking risk across ~60% of China’s land during summer peaks, with increasing risk under future warming, suggests an analysis of 4,000 laboratory and field records from 399 global sites and an interpretable artificial intelligence framework. Soil cracking disrupts water regulation, weakens landscapes, and accelerates carbon loss, yet its implications for sustainability under climate change remain poorly understood. Here we present a nationwide assessment of spatiotemporal patterns and drivers of soil cracking across China. Using over 4,000 laboratory and field records from 399 global sites, we developed an interpretable artificial intelligence framework to identify the dominant controls and map current and future risks. Soil plasticity and water content emerged as the principal predictors. Results for 2022 reveal pronounced seasonal cycles, with summer peaks affecting nearly 60% of China’s land, and hotspots in the Loess Plateau, North China, and Northwest China. Future projections under climate scenarios show >5% expansion of land at high risk and >10% intensification of cracking in northern and northwestern regions. These findings highlight soil cracking as a systemic but overlooked climate-driven hazard with cascading consequences for land degradation, ecosystem resilience, and sustainable development. Published: 2026-07-13T09:03:21+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.435 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ting Wang; Chao-Sheng Tang; Zhixiong Zeng; Farshid Vahedifard; Qing Cheng; Ben-Gang Tian; Jin-Jian Xu; Zhengtao Shen; Bin Shi

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03810-7

Relevance: 0.435 (flagship)

Soil plasticity and water content drive elevated soil cracking risk across ~60% of China’s land during summer peaks, with increasing risk under future warming, suggests an analysis of 4,000 laboratory and field records from 399 global sites and an interpretable artificial intelligence framework. Soil cracking disrupts water regulation, weakens landscapes, and accelerates carbon loss, yet its implications for sustainability under climate change remain poorly understood. Here we present a nationwide assessment of spatiotemporal patterns and drivers of soil cracking across China. Using over 4,000 laboratory and field records from 399 global sites, we developed an interpretable artificial intelligence framework to identify the dominant controls and map current and future risks. Soil plasticity and water content emerged as the principal predictors. Results for 2022 reveal pronounced seasonal cycles, with summer peaks affecting nearly 60% of China’s land, and hotspots in the Loess Plateau, North China, and Northwest China. Future projections under climate scenarios show >5% expansion of land at high risk and >10% intensification of cracking in northern and northwestern regions. These findings highlight soil cracking as a systemic but overlooked climate-driven hazard with cascading consequences for land degradation, ecosystem resilience, and sustainable development.

item.title: Light hydrogen isotopes in terrestrial core
item.dc:creator: Yu Zhang
Wenzhong Wang
Zhengbin Deng
Zhongqing Wu
Bowen Chen
item.description: The origin of Earth’s water remains unresolved. Hydrogen isotopes provide a key tracer of planetary water history, yet the isotope composition of Earth’s core—the planet’s largest internal reservoir—has remained unconstrained. Here, we use first-principles calculations combined with machine-learning–accelerated path-integral molecular dynamics to quantify hydrogen isotope fractionation between silicate and metallic melts under core-forming conditions. We find that core formation enriched the silicate Earth in deuterium while concentrating isotopically light hydrogen in the core, requiring the proto-Earth to have started with a lower deuterium-to-hydrogen (D/H) ratio than that preserves in the present-day mantle. Our models show that the bulk-Earth D/H ratio can be explained either by direct accretion of enstatite chondrite–dominated material or by isotopic resetting of deuterium-rich planetesimals through interactions with solar nebular gas. These results suggest that Earth’s water inventory was established during the earliest stages of accretion. Published: 2026-07-10T17:58:37+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.572 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yu Zhang; Wenzhong Wang; Zhengbin Deng; Zhongqing Wu; Bowen Chen

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aec5685

Relevance: 0.572 (flagship)

The origin of Earth’s water remains unresolved. Hydrogen isotopes provide a key tracer of planetary water history, yet the isotope composition of Earth’s core—the planet’s largest internal reservoir—has remained unconstrained. Here, we use first-principles calculations combined with machine-learning–accelerated path-integral molecular dynamics to quantify hydrogen isotope fractionation between silicate and metallic melts under core-forming conditions. We find that core formation enriched the silicate Earth in deuterium while concentrating isotopically light hydrogen in the core, requiring the proto-Earth to have started with a lower deuterium-to-hydrogen (D/H) ratio than that preserves in the present-day mantle. Our models show that the bulk-Earth D/H ratio can be explained either by direct accretion of enstatite chondrite–dominated material or by isotopic resetting of deuterium-rich planetesimals through interactions with solar nebular gas. These results suggest that Earth’s water inventory was established during the earliest stages of accretion.

item.title: Barium senses subtle pore changes in a voltage-gated K <sup>+</sup> channel associated with voltage sensor states and regulatory subunits
item.dc:creator: Lei Huang
Nitzan Daus
Yuyin Wang
Guohui Zhang
Manar Khier
Ariel Ben-Bassat
Jingyi Shi
Lu Zhao
Borui Zhang
Lu Han
Yoni Haitin
Bernard Attali
Jianmin Cui
item.description: In classic models of voltage-dependent channel activation, the pore only opens after the voltage sensor domains (VSDs) activate in an “all-or-none” fashion. Whether the VSD alters open pore properties remains unclear. Here, we examine the properties of the KCNQ1 channel pore in relation with the VSD and regulatory subunits that are located outside of the pore using barium ion (Ba 2+ ) block as a probe. We find that the external Ba 2+ block of KCNQ1 channels is voltage-dependent, and the voltage-dependent channel opening in Ba 2+ mainly derives from voltage-dependent Ba 2+ unblock. Open pore conformational changes indicated by the voltage dependence of Ba 2+ unblock vary with the states of the VSD, the alterations of VSD-pore coupling, and the association with regulatory KCNE subunits. Contrary to the classic view, our results suggest that, instead of being independent of the voltage gating machinery, the pore of KCNQ1 is flexible and influenced by the subtle changes in the voltage sensor state and the environment outside of the pore. Published: 2026-07-10T17:58:37+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.421 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lei Huang; Nitzan Daus; Yuyin Wang; Guohui Zhang; Manar Khier; Ariel Ben-Bassat; Jingyi Shi; Lu Zhao; Borui Zhang; Lu Han; Yoni Haitin; Bernard Attali; Jianmin Cui

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aec6510

Relevance: 0.421 (flagship)

In classic models of voltage-dependent channel activation, the pore only opens after the voltage sensor domains (VSDs) activate in an “all-or-none” fashion. Whether the VSD alters open pore properties remains unclear. Here, we examine the properties of the KCNQ1 channel pore in relation with the VSD and regulatory subunits that are located outside of the pore using barium ion (Ba 2+ ) block as a probe. We find that the external Ba 2+ block of KCNQ1 channels is voltage-dependent, and the voltage-dependent channel opening in Ba 2+ mainly derives from voltage-dependent Ba 2+ unblock. Open pore conformational changes indicated by the voltage dependence of Ba 2+ unblock vary with the states of the VSD, the alterations of VSD-pore coupling, and the association with regulatory KCNE subunits. Contrary to the classic view, our results suggest that, instead of being independent of the voltage gating machinery, the pore of KCNQ1 is flexible and influenced by the subtle changes in the voltage sensor state and the environment outside of the pore.

item.title: Structural evolution of iron oxides melts at Earth’s outer-core pressures
item.dc:creator: Céline Crépisson
Mila Fitzgerald
Domenic Peake
Patrick G. Heighway
Thomas Stevens
Adrien Descamps
David McGonegle
Alexis Amouretti
Karim K. Alaa El-Din
Michal Andrzejewski
Sam Azadi
Erik Brambrink
Carolina Camarda
David A. Chin
Samuele Di Dio Cafiso
Ana Coutinho Dutra
Hauke Höppner
Kohdai Yamamoto
Phani S. Karamched
Zuzana Konôpková
Motoaki Nakatsutsumi
Norimasa Ozaki
Danae N. Polsin
Jan-Patrick Schwinkendorf
Georgiy Shoulga
Cornelius Strohm
Minxue Tang
Harry Taylor
Monika Toncian
Yizhen Wang
Jin Yao
Gianluca Gregori
Justin S. Wark
Karen Appel
Marion Harmand
Sam M. Vinko
item.description: Abstract Oxygen and other light elements comprise up to 5 wt% of the Earth’s outer-core, and may significantly influence its physical properties and the operation of the geodynamo. Here we report in situ X-ray diffraction measurements of Fe, Fe + 4.5 FeO (atomic proportion), and Fe 2 O 3 melts at 177-440 GPa, achieved using laser-driven shock compression at an x-ray free-electron laser. The melts exhibit Fe-O coordination numbers between 4.0(0.4) and 4.5(0.4), indicating predominantly four-fold coordination environments. These coordination states are significantly smaller than those of Fe-bearing lower-mantle phases such as bridgmanite and ferropericlase. Shorter Fe-Fe interatomic distances in compressed iron oxide melts drive the denser packing relative to ambient melts, while the structural differences between Fe + 4.5 FeO and Fe 2 O 3 melts under shock indicate that the oxidation state modulates oxygen solubility in liquid Fe. At 177 GPa ( ~ 380 km below the core-mantle boundary) and 3800 K, Fe 2 O 3 melts exhibit higher Fe-O coordination, suggesting that local variations in oxygen content could contribute to the stratification in the uppermost outer-core inferred from seismological and geomagnetic observations. Published: 2026-07-10T11:45:39+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.566 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Céline Crépisson; Mila Fitzgerald; Domenic Peake; Patrick G. Heighway; Thomas Stevens; Adrien Descamps; David McGonegle; Alexis Amouretti; Karim K. Alaa El-Din; Michal Andrzejewski; Sam Azadi; Erik Brambrink; Carolina Camarda; David A. Chin; Samuele Di Dio Cafiso; Ana Coutinho Dutra; Hauke Höppner; Kohdai Yamamoto; Phani S. Karamched; Zuzana Konôpková; Motoaki Nakatsutsumi; Norimasa Ozaki; Danae N. Polsin; Jan-Patrick Schwinkendorf; Georgiy Shoulga; Cornelius Strohm; Minxue Tang; Harry Taylor; Monika Toncian; Yizhen Wang; Jin Yao; Gianluca Gregori; Justin S. Wark; Karen Appel; Marion Harmand; Sam M. Vinko

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-75204-4

Relevance: 0.566 (flagship)

Abstract Oxygen and other light elements comprise up to 5 wt% of the Earth’s outer-core, and may significantly influence its physical properties and the operation of the geodynamo. Here we report in situ X-ray diffraction measurements of Fe, Fe + 4.5 FeO (atomic proportion), and Fe 2 O 3 melts at 177-440 GPa, achieved using laser-driven shock compression at an x-ray free-electron laser. The melts exhibit Fe-O coordination numbers between 4.0(0.4) and 4.5(0.4), indicating predominantly four-fold coordination environments. These coordination states are significantly smaller than those of Fe-bearing lower-mantle phases such as bridgmanite and ferropericlase. Shorter Fe-Fe interatomic distances in compressed iron oxide melts drive the denser packing relative to ambient melts, while the structural differences between Fe + 4.5 FeO and Fe 2 O 3 melts under shock indicate that the oxidation state modulates oxygen solubility in liquid Fe. At 177 GPa ( ~ 380 km below the core-mantle boundary) and 3800 K, Fe 2 O 3 melts exhibit higher Fe-O coordination, suggesting that local variations in oxygen content could contribute to the stratification in the uppermost outer-core inferred from seismological and geomagnetic observations.

item.title: Hydroclimate controls Congo peatland net oxygen release over the past 10,600 years
item.dc:creator: M. E. Galvez
S. Wu
Y. Garcin
E. Schefuß
G. Gassier
J. Lebamba
C. K. Kiahtipes
F. Bokomba Bwamangele
R. Kidebua Lutonadio
H.-P. Wotzka
T. Adatte
S. L. Jaccard
item.description: Abstract The burial of organic matter in marine environments is considered the dominant long-term source of atmospheric oxygen, yet the contribution of terrestrial burial remains poorly constrained. Quantifying any oxygen surplus associated with organic matter burial conventionally assumes a 1:1 oxygen-to-carbon stoichiometry. Here we aim to test this assumption and quantify net oxygen release from a terrestrial setting over time. We applied direct redox titration to a 10,600-year Congo Basin peat core, a hotspot of carbon accumulation. We find systematic deviation from the 1:1 ratio, enabling precise, time-resolved quantification of the net redox imbalance. Fluxes were strikingly nonlinear: drier phases triggered abrupt ~80% declines in net oxygen surplus within centuries, followed by rapid recovery when wetter conditions returned, revealing a hydroclimate-sensitive carbon–oxygen valve. Cumulative Holocene oxygen surplus reached 83 [68–100] petagrams of oxygen equivalents. Using the Congo oxygen-to-carbon ratio, we estimate that global peatland net oxygen production is commensurate with continental weathering oxygen sinks in the Holocene—a flux comparable in magnitude to marine burial but responding on orders-of-magnitude-shorter timescales. This finding provides qualitative insight into the contribution of terrestrial environments to Earth’s redox balance. It also offers a direct stoichiometric framework—applicable to coal archives—for quantifying that contribution across the Phanerozoic, independent of isotopic proxies, complementing existing marine-based models. Published: 2026-07-10T09:02:12+00:00 Venue: Nature Geoscience Score: 0.543 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: M. E. Galvez; S. Wu; Y. Garcin; E. Schefuß; G. Gassier; J. Lebamba; C. K. Kiahtipes; F. Bokomba Bwamangele; R. Kidebua Lutonadio; H.-P. Wotzka; T. Adatte; S. L. Jaccard

Journal: Nature Geoscience

DOI: 10.1038/s41561-026-02031-z

Relevance: 0.543 (flagship)

Abstract The burial of organic matter in marine environments is considered the dominant long-term source of atmospheric oxygen, yet the contribution of terrestrial burial remains poorly constrained. Quantifying any oxygen surplus associated with organic matter burial conventionally assumes a 1:1 oxygen-to-carbon stoichiometry. Here we aim to test this assumption and quantify net oxygen release from a terrestrial setting over time. We applied direct redox titration to a 10,600-year Congo Basin peat core, a hotspot of carbon accumulation. We find systematic deviation from the 1:1 ratio, enabling precise, time-resolved quantification of the net redox imbalance. Fluxes were strikingly nonlinear: drier phases triggered abrupt ~80% declines in net oxygen surplus within centuries, followed by rapid recovery when wetter conditions returned, revealing a hydroclimate-sensitive carbon–oxygen valve. Cumulative Holocene oxygen surplus reached 83 [68–100] petagrams of oxygen equivalents. Using the Congo oxygen-to-carbon ratio, we estimate that global peatland net oxygen production is commensurate with continental weathering oxygen sinks in the Holocene—a flux comparable in magnitude to marine burial but responding on orders-of-magnitude-shorter timescales. This finding provides qualitative insight into the contribution of terrestrial environments to Earth’s redox balance. It also offers a direct stoichiometric framework—applicable to coal archives—for quantifying that contribution across the Phanerozoic, independent of isotopic proxies, complementing existing marine-based models.

item.title: West versus Central Tibet exhumation difference influenced by Indian slab underthrusting
item.dc:creator: Weiwei Xue
Yani Najman
Xiumian Hu
Cristina Persano
Finlay M. Stuart
Mark Wildman
Anlin Ma
Yida Yang
Gong-Jian Tang
Lin Gong
Qiang Wang
item.description: The extent of Indian continental underthrusting beneath Western Tibet influences its moderate-to-rapid exhumation, relative to slow exhumation in Central Tibet, according to thermochronological data and exhumation history modelling. The Tibetan Plateau has an important role in controlling the Asian monsoon, river drainage and biodiversity patterns across Asia. The history of plateau development and its mechanistic links to underthrusting of the Indian plate beneath the Asian plate remain controversial. Here, using mid- to low-temperature thermochronological data and exhumation history modelling, we show differences in the exhumation history of Central and Western Tibet during the middle Cenozoic. Central Tibet has experienced slow exhumation from ~45 million years ago (Ma) to the present while Western Tibet underwent moderate-to-rapid exhumation between 45 and 20 Ma at a rate that exceeds the modern value by an order of magnitude. Movement on large strike-slip faults and climatic forcing can be excluded as the primary reason for the regional exhumation difference. Using bedrock cooling ages and the eruption age of (ultra)potassic lavas as proxies for early exhumation and continental underthrusting, respectively, and combining these with geophysical imaging data, we propose that the spatial differences in exhumation rates at 45–20 Ma are largely attributed to the extent of Indian continental underthrusting, with Western Tibet lying within the underthrust domain and Central Tibet lying outside it. These findings highlight continental underthrusting as a first-order control on collisional plateau development. Published: 2026-07-10T09:01:51+00:00 Venue: Nature Geoscience Score: 0.580 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Weiwei Xue; Yani Najman; Xiumian Hu; Cristina Persano; Finlay M. Stuart; Mark Wildman; Anlin Ma; Yida Yang; Gong-Jian Tang; Lin Gong; Qiang Wang

Journal: Nature Geoscience

DOI: 10.1038/s41561-026-02043-9

Relevance: 0.580 (flagship)

The extent of Indian continental underthrusting beneath Western Tibet influences its moderate-to-rapid exhumation, relative to slow exhumation in Central Tibet, according to thermochronological data and exhumation history modelling. The Tibetan Plateau has an important role in controlling the Asian monsoon, river drainage and biodiversity patterns across Asia. The history of plateau development and its mechanistic links to underthrusting of the Indian plate beneath the Asian plate remain controversial. Here, using mid- to low-temperature thermochronological data and exhumation history modelling, we show differences in the exhumation history of Central and Western Tibet during the middle Cenozoic. Central Tibet has experienced slow exhumation from ~45 million years ago (Ma) to the present while Western Tibet underwent moderate-to-rapid exhumation between 45 and 20 Ma at a rate that exceeds the modern value by an order of magnitude. Movement on large strike-slip faults and climatic forcing can be excluded as the primary reason for the regional exhumation difference. Using bedrock cooling ages and the eruption age of (ultra)potassic lavas as proxies for early exhumation and continental underthrusting, respectively, and combining these with geophysical imaging data, we propose that the spatial differences in exhumation rates at 45–20 Ma are largely attributed to the extent of Indian continental underthrusting, with Western Tibet lying within the underthrust domain and Central Tibet lying outside it. These findings highlight continental underthrusting as a first-order control on collisional plateau development.

item.title: A single freeze cycle redirects iron mineral transformation
item.dc:creator: Tao Luo
Tao Chen
Tra My Bui Thi
James Behan
Crispin Hetherington
Khalil Hanna
Jean-François Boily
item.description: Polycrystalline ice formation concentrates mineral nanoparticles into liquid boundaries between growing ice crystals. Here we show that minutes of freezing dictate iron mineral fate over subsequent months of aqueous aging. A single freeze–thaw cycle irreversibly aggregates ferrihydrite through converging physical and chemical mechanisms. Freeze concentration collapses electrostatic barriers while cryosuction strips hydration layers and compresses nanoparticles into micrometer-scale planar aggregates. Chemical evidence points to interfacial (hydr)oxo bridging, alongside hydrogen bonding, that resists disaggregation. These mechanisms lock nanoparticles into mesocrystal-like assemblages that retain their nanoscale identity but inhibit dissolution–reprecipitation to goethite, instead favoring solid-state transformation to hematite. Ice formation thus acts as a geochemical reactor, driving aggregation and interfacial bonding that redirect iron speciation, with broad implications for nutrient cycling and carbon preservation across the cryosphere. Published: 2026-07-09T18:02:13+00:00 Venue: Science Score: 0.542 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tao Luo; Tao Chen; Tra My Bui Thi; James Behan; Crispin Hetherington; Khalil Hanna; Jean-François Boily

Journal: Science

DOI: 10.1126/science.aee9519

Relevance: 0.542 (flagship)

Polycrystalline ice formation concentrates mineral nanoparticles into liquid boundaries between growing ice crystals. Here we show that minutes of freezing dictate iron mineral fate over subsequent months of aqueous aging. A single freeze–thaw cycle irreversibly aggregates ferrihydrite through converging physical and chemical mechanisms. Freeze concentration collapses electrostatic barriers while cryosuction strips hydration layers and compresses nanoparticles into micrometer-scale planar aggregates. Chemical evidence points to interfacial (hydr)oxo bridging, alongside hydrogen bonding, that resists disaggregation. These mechanisms lock nanoparticles into mesocrystal-like assemblages that retain their nanoscale identity but inhibit dissolution–reprecipitation to goethite, instead favoring solid-state transformation to hematite. Ice formation thus acts as a geochemical reactor, driving aggregation and interfacial bonding that redirect iron speciation, with broad implications for nutrient cycling and carbon preservation across the cryosphere.

item.title: Emerging insights on oceanic intraplate volcanism
item.description: Recent research suggests that most forms of volcanism within oceanic plates are influenced by plate tectonics, particularly via the involvement of tectonics in whole-mantle dynamics. Investigating oceanic intraplate volcanism may therefore advance our understanding of geodynamic processes. Published: 2026-07-09T15:01:13+00:00 Venue: Nature Geoscience Score: 0.566 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Journal: Nature Geoscience

DOI: 10.1038/s41561-026-02051-9

Relevance: 0.566 (flagship)

Recent research suggests that most forms of volcanism within oceanic plates are influenced by plate tectonics, particularly via the involvement of tectonics in whole-mantle dynamics. Investigating oceanic intraplate volcanism may therefore advance our understanding of geodynamic processes.

item.title: Pathways to cost competitive and viable lithium production from Salton Sea geothermal brines
item.dc:creator: Jannis Wesselkaemper
Theo Renaud
Naod Araya
Ken Dekkers
Joris Popineau
Jeremy Riffault
John O’Sullivan
Andrew Z. Haddad
item.description: Abstract Lithium supply chains remain heavily concentrated in hard rock and brine resources, creating significant supply risks. Geothermal brines represent an underutilized alternative, yet commercial progress is hindered by the absence of facility-scale cost assessments. Here, we present a techno-economic analysis of large-scale lithium extraction from Salton Sea geothermal brines, drawing on primary company disclosures, process patents, and brine resource modeling. Caused by varying lithium and impurity concentrations, brine dilution over time, and process configurations (e.g., production via carbonation and conversion vs. electrolysis), we find that large-scale production costs may reach ~10,000 United States dollars per ton, but increase up to 22,000 United States dollars per ton with higher certainty of brine modeling, raising concerns about economic competitiveness to conventional low-cost sources. Finally, a project feasibility-focused scenario analyses shows that leveraging brine pre-treatment by-product sales could lower long-term lithium break-even prices by ~5,000 United States dollars per ton, whereas capital cost optimization of 20% could further reduce lithium break-even prices by 10%. Published: 2026-07-09T09:25:06+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.504 (flagship)

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-75389-8

Relevance: 0.504 (flagship)

Abstract Lithium supply chains remain heavily concentrated in hard rock and brine resources, creating significant supply risks. Geothermal brines represent an underutilized alternative, yet commercial progress is hindered by the absence of facility-scale cost assessments. Here, we present a techno-economic analysis of large-scale lithium extraction from Salton Sea geothermal brines, drawing on primary company disclosures, process patents, and brine resource modeling. Caused by varying lithium and impurity concentrations, brine dilution over time, and process configurations (e.g., production via carbonation and conversion vs. electrolysis), we find that large-scale production costs may reach ~10,000 United States dollars per ton, but increase up to 22,000 United States dollars per ton with higher certainty of brine modeling, raising concerns about economic competitiveness to conventional low-cost sources. Finally, a project feasibility-focused scenario analyses shows that leveraging brine pre-treatment by-product sales could lower long-term lithium break-even prices by ~5,000 United States dollars per ton, whereas capital cost optimization of 20% could further reduce lithium break-even prices by 10%.

item.title: Mineralized carbonates contribute to the millennial durability of Roman concrete
item.dc:creator: Xiaohong Zhu
Sejung Rosie Chae
Stuart McElhany
Chengyao Liang
Qi Zheng
Jiaqi Li
Veronica Fondi
Sergio Del Ferro
Ascanio Modena Altieri
Harrison P. Lisabeth
Arun Bhattacharjee
Bruce W. Fouke
Hans-Rudolf Wenk
Paulo J. M. Monteiro
item.description: Roman concrete structures have remained serviceable for nearly two millennia and are widely regarded as outstanding examples of durable ancient engineering. Existing literature attributes Roman concrete longevity to the pozzolanic reaction that occurs between reactive volcanic ashes and lime. While the pozzolanic reaction is of fundamental importance, we argue that carbonation over a long period of time also substantially enhances the durability and potential self-healing properties of concrete. To validate this claim, a comprehensive analysis of the concrete used in a latrine included in the Canopus western substructures, in Hadrian’s Villa (Roman imperial residence, Tivoli, Italy, 2nd century AD), is performed using multiscale spectroscopic and tomographic approaches to uncover the mechanisms behind the resilience of these enduring structures. Microstructural evidence reveals that volcanic lava composed of leucite, analcime, and ferrian diopside was combined by the Romans with lime at a water-to-binder ratio of ~0.4 to 0.45 to mix the concrete. Calcite cementation in pore spaces and fractures served as the primary binding mineral phase, while the formation of a relatively minor amount of calcium-aluminum-silicate-hydrate precipitated around and between lava fragments to further enhance the integrity of concrete. Conversely, unhydrated calcium oxide reacted with atmospheric carbon dioxide and moisture to form volumetrically dominant calcite cements, creating the primary driver to reinforce structural strength and occlude porosity. This radiaxial fibrous calcite has the potential to mitigate environmental and mechanical stresses in modern concrete infrastructure and advance the development of sustainable and resilient construction materials in the future. Published: 2026-07-08T18:00:55+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.504 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaohong Zhu; Sejung Rosie Chae; Stuart McElhany; Chengyao Liang; Qi Zheng; Jiaqi Li; Veronica Fondi; Sergio Del Ferro; Ascanio Modena Altieri; Harrison P. Lisabeth; Arun Bhattacharjee; Bruce W. Fouke; Hans-Rudolf Wenk; Paulo J. M. Monteiro

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aeb0754

Relevance: 0.504 (flagship)

Roman concrete structures have remained serviceable for nearly two millennia and are widely regarded as outstanding examples of durable ancient engineering. Existing literature attributes Roman concrete longevity to the pozzolanic reaction that occurs between reactive volcanic ashes and lime. While the pozzolanic reaction is of fundamental importance, we argue that carbonation over a long period of time also substantially enhances the durability and potential self-healing properties of concrete. To validate this claim, a comprehensive analysis of the concrete used in a latrine included in the Canopus western substructures, in Hadrian’s Villa (Roman imperial residence, Tivoli, Italy, 2nd century AD), is performed using multiscale spectroscopic and tomographic approaches to uncover the mechanisms behind the resilience of these enduring structures. Microstructural evidence reveals that volcanic lava composed of leucite, analcime, and ferrian diopside was combined by the Romans with lime at a water-to-binder ratio of ~0.4 to 0.45 to mix the concrete. Calcite cementation in pore spaces and fractures served as the primary binding mineral phase, while the formation of a relatively minor amount of calcium-aluminum-silicate-hydrate precipitated around and between lava fragments to further enhance the integrity of concrete. Conversely, unhydrated calcium oxide reacted with atmospheric carbon dioxide and moisture to form volumetrically dominant calcite cements, creating the primary driver to reinforce structural strength and occlude porosity. This radiaxial fibrous calcite has the potential to mitigate environmental and mechanical stresses in modern concrete infrastructure and advance the development of sustainable and resilient construction materials in the future.

item.title: Deep magma underpressure and connectivity drive large dike intrusions
item.dc:creator: Carolina Pagli
Alessandro La Rosa
Simone Cesca
Torsten Dahm
Hua Wang
Manuela Bonano
Pasquale Striano
Pinar Büyükakpınar
Eleonora Rivalta
Derek Keir
Francesco Casu
Atalay Ayele
Elias Lewi
item.description: Large dikes are the main mechanism of crustal extension in volcanic areas, but the processes in the underlying magma system that supply the required volumes remain unclear. We show that 1.4 cubic kilometers of magma propagated under the Ethiopian rift in December 2024 and continued for ~3 months. Geodesy and seismicity reveal that the dike was fed from a network of magma reservoirs between 6 to 12 kilometers in depth with pathways rapidly forming between them. We calculate pressure changes in the reservoirs and show that underpressure developed in the deeper portion, creating the conditions to drain large magma volumes. We find that tectonic stress and availability of magma alone are not enough to drive intrusion of massive dikes. These events will start only after magma connectivity and deep underpressure develop. Similar conditions may be important for the transfer of large magma volumes from the mantle and the formation of large igneous provinces. Published: 2026-07-08T18:00:55+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.544 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Carolina Pagli; Alessandro La Rosa; Simone Cesca; Torsten Dahm; Hua Wang; Manuela Bonano; Pasquale Striano; Pinar Büyükakpınar; Eleonora Rivalta; Derek Keir; Francesco Casu; Atalay Ayele; Elias Lewi

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aed6073

Relevance: 0.544 (flagship)

Large dikes are the main mechanism of crustal extension in volcanic areas, but the processes in the underlying magma system that supply the required volumes remain unclear. We show that 1.4 cubic kilometers of magma propagated under the Ethiopian rift in December 2024 and continued for ~3 months. Geodesy and seismicity reveal that the dike was fed from a network of magma reservoirs between 6 to 12 kilometers in depth with pathways rapidly forming between them. We calculate pressure changes in the reservoirs and show that underpressure developed in the deeper portion, creating the conditions to drain large magma volumes. We find that tectonic stress and availability of magma alone are not enough to drive intrusion of massive dikes. These events will start only after magma connectivity and deep underpressure develop. Similar conditions may be important for the transfer of large magma volumes from the mantle and the formation of large igneous provinces.

item.title: Counterfactual modeling isolates sand mining impacts, revealing it as a key driver of Mekong Delta destabilization
item.dc:creator: Sonu Kumar
Edward Park
Dung Duc Tran
Thanh Quoc Vo
Karl Kästner
Sameh Kantoush
Doan Van Binh
Jiachun Huang
Jingyu Wang
Adam D. Switzer
item.description: Sand mining is a poorly quantified threat to river deltas because its impacts are often confounded with those of dams and climate change. Here, we isolate its effects in the Vietnamese Mekong Delta, a global sand mining hot spot, using a long-term numerical model and observed mining data. Results show that mining exceeds natural sediment supply by 6 to 15 times, causing riverbed erosion across ~65% of channels, with mean incision rates of ~0.10 meters per year (~25 to 30% of total incision driven by all drivers). This channel deepening alters flow, reduces sediment transport, and enhances saltwater intrusion. Sand mining alone contributes ~16 to 30% of the annual salinity increase, extending intrusion up to ~1.5 kilometers further inland during the dry season. These changes demonstrate that sand mining is a major, previously underquantified driver of delta instability, affecting river morphology, flow, sediment, and salinity. This study provides a framework to quantify these impacts and support better management of sand extraction in vulnerable deltas worldwide. Published: 2026-07-08T18:00:55+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.447 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sonu Kumar; Edward Park; Dung Duc Tran; Thanh Quoc Vo; Karl Kästner; Sameh Kantoush; Doan Van Binh; Jiachun Huang; Jingyu Wang; Adam D. Switzer

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aef0558

Relevance: 0.447 (flagship)

Sand mining is a poorly quantified threat to river deltas because its impacts are often confounded with those of dams and climate change. Here, we isolate its effects in the Vietnamese Mekong Delta, a global sand mining hot spot, using a long-term numerical model and observed mining data. Results show that mining exceeds natural sediment supply by 6 to 15 times, causing riverbed erosion across ~65% of channels, with mean incision rates of ~0.10 meters per year (~25 to 30% of total incision driven by all drivers). This channel deepening alters flow, reduces sediment transport, and enhances saltwater intrusion. Sand mining alone contributes ~16 to 30% of the annual salinity increase, extending intrusion up to ~1.5 kilometers further inland during the dry season. These changes demonstrate that sand mining is a major, previously underquantified driver of delta instability, affecting river morphology, flow, sediment, and salinity. This study provides a framework to quantify these impacts and support better management of sand extraction in vulnerable deltas worldwide.

item.title: Propagation of fluid-driven aseismic slip fronts under upper crustal conditions
item.dc:creator: François Passelègue
Nicolas Brantut
Hervé Chauris
Pierre Dublanchet
item.description: Fracture mechanics models suggest that aseismic slip may either lag behind or outpace the fluid pressure front, depending on injection conditions and the fault’s initial stress state. However, direct experimental validation of these predictions has been lacking. Here, we report laboratory experiments on granite samples under upper crustal stress conditions, where we simultaneously tracked the propagation of fluid pressure and aseismic slip during fluid injection. We show that aseismic slip lags the pressure front at low injection rates and low initial stress but rapidly outpaces it under high-rate injection or near-failure initial stress conditions. The transition is governed by a dimensionless loading parameter that integrates injection rate, fault strength, and initial shear stress. Our results provide direct support for fracture mechanics models of fluid-induced aseismic slip and suggest that in critically stressed crustal faults, rupture propagation may commonly outpace fluid diffusion. Published: 2026-07-08T18:00:55+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.553 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: François Passelègue; Nicolas Brantut; Hervé Chauris; Pierre Dublanchet

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aeb0234

Relevance: 0.553 (flagship)

Fracture mechanics models suggest that aseismic slip may either lag behind or outpace the fluid pressure front, depending on injection conditions and the fault’s initial stress state. However, direct experimental validation of these predictions has been lacking. Here, we report laboratory experiments on granite samples under upper crustal stress conditions, where we simultaneously tracked the propagation of fluid pressure and aseismic slip during fluid injection. We show that aseismic slip lags the pressure front at low injection rates and low initial stress but rapidly outpaces it under high-rate injection or near-failure initial stress conditions. The transition is governed by a dimensionless loading parameter that integrates injection rate, fault strength, and initial shear stress. Our results provide direct support for fracture mechanics models of fluid-induced aseismic slip and suggest that in critically stressed crustal faults, rupture propagation may commonly outpace fluid diffusion.

item.title: Anatomy of a seafloor spreading event captured by in situ seismogeodesy
item.dc:creator: Jean-Yves Royer
Jean-Arthur Olive
Sara Bazin
Valérie Ballu
Anne Briais
Lise Retailleau
Pierre-Yves Raumer
Edgar Lenhof
J. Beesau
R. Daniel
D. Dausse
S. Furst
A. Gros-Martial
C. Guerin
E. Klein
D. Pacaud
C. Poitou
J. Tanrin
L. Testut
item.description: Abstract Over geological time, the growth of the ocean floor involves magmatic and tectonic extension 1 at mid-ocean ridges (MORs). Because seismogeodetic monitoring of these submarine plate boundaries remains challenging 2–7 , little is known about how these systems operate on yearly timescales. Here we report the first, to our knowledge, in situ observation of a rifting event at a MOR segment that combines hydroacoustic, direct-path ranging and bottom-pressure measurements, with repeated seafloor mapping. This event started on 26 April 2024 at the axis of the Southeast Indian Ridge (SEIR) near 37° S, two months after instruments had been deployed across the ridge axis and nearby Amsterdam transform fault (TF). The event began as a rapidly migrating swarm of extensional seismicity along the axial valley. It caused 4 m of subsidence of the valley floor and more than a metre of horizontal extension across the valley. We interpret this as the deflation of a sill-like reservoir feeding propagating dykes along the ridge axis. The dykes eventually led to the outpouring of about 160 million m 3 of lava at the seafloor in about 16 days, while inducing both seismic and aseismic slip on valley-bounding normal faults and finally triggering seismic activity on the abutting TFs. Large-scale aseismic slip induced by magmatic processes could therefore be the primary mechanism by which MOR normal faults accrue their displacement, which would account for their well-documented seismic deficit 8,9 . Published: 2026-07-08T15:02:00+00:00 Venue: Nature Score: 0.582 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jean-Yves Royer; Jean-Arthur Olive; Sara Bazin; Valérie Ballu; Anne Briais; Lise Retailleau; Pierre-Yves Raumer; Edgar Lenhof; J. Beesau; R. Daniel; D. Dausse; S. Furst; A. Gros-Martial; C. Guerin; E. Klein; D. Pacaud; C. Poitou; J. Tanrin; L. Testut

Journal: Nature

DOI: 10.1038/s41586-026-10785-0

Relevance: 0.582 (flagship)

Abstract Over geological time, the growth of the ocean floor involves magmatic and tectonic extension 1 at mid-ocean ridges (MORs). Because seismogeodetic monitoring of these submarine plate boundaries remains challenging 2–7 , little is known about how these systems operate on yearly timescales. Here we report the first, to our knowledge, in situ observation of a rifting event at a MOR segment that combines hydroacoustic, direct-path ranging and bottom-pressure measurements, with repeated seafloor mapping. This event started on 26 April 2024 at the axis of the Southeast Indian Ridge (SEIR) near 37° S, two months after instruments had been deployed across the ridge axis and nearby Amsterdam transform fault (TF). The event began as a rapidly migrating swarm of extensional seismicity along the axial valley. It caused 4 m of subsidence of the valley floor and more than a metre of horizontal extension across the valley. We interpret this as the deflation of a sill-like reservoir feeding propagating dykes along the ridge axis. The dykes eventually led to the outpouring of about 160 million m 3 of lava at the seafloor in about 16 days, while inducing both seismic and aseismic slip on valley-bounding normal faults and finally triggering seismic activity on the abutting TFs. Large-scale aseismic slip induced by magmatic processes could therefore be the primary mechanism by which MOR normal faults accrue their displacement, which would account for their well-documented seismic deficit 8,9 .

item.title: Dual clumped isotopes in foraminiferal calcite reveal kinetic bias in some species
item.dc:creator: Amelia Jane Davies
Marion Peral
Jonathan Erez
Adam Levi
David Evans
Oliver Friedrich
Thibaut Caley
Romi Nambiar
Douglas Coenen
Philip Tauxe Staudigel
Mattia Tagliavento
Miguel Bernecker
Jens Fiebig
item.description: Abstract Clumped isotope (∆ 47 ) analyses of foraminifera provide a powerful means to reconstruct upper and bottom water temperatures, independent of seawater δ 18 O and elemental composition. We show statistically significant disequilibrium in the dual (∆ 47 -∆ 48 ) clumped isotope composition of some large benthic foraminifera, while other large benthic foraminifera plot within analytical uncertainty of ∆ 47 –∆ 48 equilibrium. This deviation suggests the influence of reaction kinetics, such as kinetic effects during CO 2 absorption or a combination of ion attachment/detachment effects at the solution-crystal interface and slight DIC disequilibrium. Measured core-top, planktic and deep-sea benthic foraminifera plot within the 95% confidence level of empirical ∆ 47 -∆ 48 equilibrium, although additional measurements are required to confidently exclude kinetic ∆ 47 -∆ 48 bias. Dual clumped isotope measurements of large benthic foraminifera from the Eocene of the Paris and Hampshire basins that conform with ∆ 47 -∆ 48 equilibrium (95% confidence level) further support existing evidence for surface ocean temperatures ~12–17 °C warmer than today. Published: 2026-07-08T09:12:51+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.500 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Amelia Jane Davies; Marion Peral; Jonathan Erez; Adam Levi; David Evans; Oliver Friedrich; Thibaut Caley; Romi Nambiar; Douglas Coenen; Philip Tauxe Staudigel; Mattia Tagliavento; Miguel Bernecker; Jens Fiebig

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03770-y

Relevance: 0.500 (flagship)

Abstract Clumped isotope (∆ 47 ) analyses of foraminifera provide a powerful means to reconstruct upper and bottom water temperatures, independent of seawater δ 18 O and elemental composition. We show statistically significant disequilibrium in the dual (∆ 47 -∆ 48 ) clumped isotope composition of some large benthic foraminifera, while other large benthic foraminifera plot within analytical uncertainty of ∆ 47 –∆ 48 equilibrium. This deviation suggests the influence of reaction kinetics, such as kinetic effects during CO 2 absorption or a combination of ion attachment/detachment effects at the solution-crystal interface and slight DIC disequilibrium. Measured core-top, planktic and deep-sea benthic foraminifera plot within the 95% confidence level of empirical ∆ 47 -∆ 48 equilibrium, although additional measurements are required to confidently exclude kinetic ∆ 47 -∆ 48 bias. Dual clumped isotope measurements of large benthic foraminifera from the Eocene of the Paris and Hampshire basins that conform with ∆ 47 -∆ 48 equilibrium (95% confidence level) further support existing evidence for surface ocean temperatures ~12–17 °C warmer than today.

item.title: Coevolution between crustal thickness and sulfur oxidation state in Gangdese felsic magmas
item.dc:creator: Xuyang Zheng
Ming Tang
Hao Chen
Ziyi Guo
item.description: Whether the abundant sulfate (S6+) of oxidized felsic magmas at convergent plate margins is sourced from subducted materials or generated through magmatic differentiation remains poorly understood. Here, we use S content in apatite to track the evolution of S redox state in felsic magmas over the past ∼120 Myr in the eastern Gangdese, southern Tibet. Unlike previous studies that rely on apatite grains separated from igneous rocks, we measure S in apatite inclusions hosted in detrital zircon. This approach enables us to assess magmatic S oxidation across the orogenic crust on a significantly broader scale than permitted by limited rock samples. Our results show two episodes of systematically elevated apatite S contents in the eastern Gangdese: one during the Late Cretaceous (95-80 Ma) and another from the Late Paleocene to the Miocene (45-10 Ma), following the India-Eurasia collision. This apatite S trend is consistent with that of the magma fO2 reconstructed using zircon Ce-U-Ti oxybarometer. More importantly, both apatite S content and magma fO2, on average, correlate with crustal thickness. While source composition and assimilation processes may contribute to redox heterogeneity in felsic magmas, our findings point to the critical role of crustal thickness in modulating the oxidation state of felsic magmas on an orogenic scale. Finally, by tracing oxidized S through detrital zircon samples, which are easy to collect and analytically efficient, our approach may allow for quick and effective evaluation of regional potential for porphyry copper mineralization. Published: 2026-07-13T21:58:49+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.627 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xuyang Zheng; Ming Tang; Hao Chen; Ziyi Guo

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120220

Relevance: 0.627 (must_read)

Whether the abundant sulfate (S6+) of oxidized felsic magmas at convergent plate margins is sourced from subducted materials or generated through magmatic differentiation remains poorly understood. Here, we use S content in apatite to track the evolution of S redox state in felsic magmas over the past ∼120 Myr in the eastern Gangdese, southern Tibet. Unlike previous studies that rely on apatite grains separated from igneous rocks, we measure S in apatite inclusions hosted in detrital zircon. This approach enables us to assess magmatic S oxidation across the orogenic crust on a significantly broader scale than permitted by limited rock samples. Our results show two episodes of systematically elevated apatite S contents in the eastern Gangdese: one during the Late Cretaceous (95-80 Ma) and another from the Late Paleocene to the Miocene (45-10 Ma), following the India-Eurasia collision. This apatite S trend is consistent with that of the magma fO2 reconstructed using zircon Ce-U-Ti oxybarometer. More importantly, both apatite S content and magma fO2, on average, correlate with crustal thickness. While source composition and assimilation processes may contribute to redox heterogeneity in felsic magmas, our findings point to the critical role of crustal thickness in modulating the oxidation state of felsic magmas on an orogenic scale. Finally, by tracing oxidized S through detrital zircon samples, which are easy to collect and analytically efficient, our approach may allow for quick and effective evaluation of regional potential for porphyry copper mineralization.

item.title: Heavy sulfur isotope of arc ultramafic cumulates revealed by S-rich apatite from the Dongwanzi intrusion, North China
item.dc:creator: Huan Jennifer Chang
Mei-Fu Zhou
Wen-Jun Hu
Shengbiao Yang
Jian-Feng Gao
item.description: Arc magmas generally have higher sulfur (S) contents and higher δ34S values than mid-ocean ridge basalts. However, it remains debated whether the enrichment of S is due to crustal processes or mantle metasomatism. We present elemental and S-Sr isotopic compositions of apatite from the Early Permian Dongwanzi ultramafic intrusion, northern margin of the North China Craton. This arc intrusion is composed of clinopyroxenite and hornblendite based on textural relationships. Clinopyroxenites contains interstitial apatite and euhedral cumulus apatite. Interstitial apatite grains have high Cl (0.36–1.10 wt%), and Th (16.1–130 ppm), and La/Yb ratios (52.1–112), high 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.70370–0.70391), and low δ34SV-CDT values (−10.5 to +4.44 ‰), consistent with crystallization from late-stage trapped melts. In contrast, euhedral cumulus apatite grains have low Cl (0.23–0.71 wt%), high δ34SV-CDT values (∼ + 7.9 ‰), and La/Yb ratios (33.4–64.6), and low initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios (∼0.70337), indicative of growth from a relatively primitive magma. Compared to cumulus apatite in clinopyroxenites, cumulus apatite grains in hornblendites contain high F (1.45–2.69 wt%) and total REE + Y (1510–9830 ppm), and high 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.70333–0.70351), but slightly low Cl (0.01–0.50 wt%) and δ34SV-CDT (7.06 ‰ to 8.25 ‰), which were attributed to progressive fractional crystallization coupled with limited wall-rock contamination from clinopyroxenite to hornblendite. As a result, the consistently high δ34S values of cumulus apatite indicate that crustal contamination only caused a limited S isotope variation (∼1 ‰) during the formation of the Dongwanzi intrusion. Using S isotopic compositions of cumulus apatite in clinopyroxenite, the parental magma has δ34S values of ∼ + 7.9 ‰. Such high δ34S values are best explained by sub-arc mantle metasomatism rather than by crustal contamination. Collectively, our study highlights that S isotopic composition of apatite can be used to track the S isotope evolution and to constrain S sources in arc mafic-ultramafic cumulates. Published: 2026-07-13T15:31:57+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.624 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Huan Jennifer Chang; Mei-Fu Zhou; Wen-Jun Hu; Shengbiao Yang; Jian-Feng Gao

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123606

Relevance: 0.624 (must_read)

Arc magmas generally have higher sulfur (S) contents and higher δ34S values than mid-ocean ridge basalts. However, it remains debated whether the enrichment of S is due to crustal processes or mantle metasomatism. We present elemental and S-Sr isotopic compositions of apatite from the Early Permian Dongwanzi ultramafic intrusion, northern margin of the North China Craton. This arc intrusion is composed of clinopyroxenite and hornblendite based on textural relationships. Clinopyroxenites contains interstitial apatite and euhedral cumulus apatite. Interstitial apatite grains have high Cl (0.36–1.10 wt%), and Th (16.1–130 ppm), and La/Yb ratios (52.1–112), high 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.70370–0.70391), and low δ34SV-CDT values (−10.5 to +4.44 ‰), consistent with crystallization from late-stage trapped melts. In contrast, euhedral cumulus apatite grains have low Cl (0.23–0.71 wt%), high δ34SV-CDT values (∼ + 7.9 ‰), and La/Yb ratios (33.4–64.6), and low initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios (∼0.70337), indicative of growth from a relatively primitive magma. Compared to cumulus apatite in clinopyroxenites, cumulus apatite grains in hornblendites contain high F (1.45–2.69 wt%) and total REE + Y (1510–9830 ppm), and high 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.70333–0.70351), but slightly low Cl (0.01–0.50 wt%) and δ34SV-CDT (7.06 ‰ to 8.25 ‰), which were attributed to progressive fractional crystallization coupled with limited wall-rock contamination from clinopyroxenite to hornblendite. As a result, the consistently high δ34S values of cumulus apatite indicate that crustal contamination only caused a limited S isotope variation (∼1 ‰) during the formation of the Dongwanzi intrusion. Using S isotopic compositions of cumulus apatite in clinopyroxenite, the parental magma has δ34S values of ∼ + 7.9 ‰. Such high δ34S values are best explained by sub-arc mantle metasomatism rather than by crustal contamination. Collectively, our study highlights that S isotopic composition of apatite can be used to track the S isotope evolution and to constrain S sources in arc mafic-ultramafic cumulates.

item.title: Genesis and geodynamic implications of Early Cretaceous granitoids in the southern Great Xing’an Range, NE China: The Laodaogou granitic complex
item.dc:creator: Guoxiong Ma
Qigui Mao
Tao Zou
Hadi Shafaii Moghadam
Yulong Jiang
Huailong Yang
Dunfeng Xiang
Meng Zhang
He Yang
Wenjiao Xiao
item.description: Mesozoic granitoids associated with Sn-Pb-Zn-Ag polymetallic deposits are prominent features in the southern Great Xing’an Range (SGXR), NE China. Understanding the origin and evolution of these granitoids is crucial for deciphering the associated mineralization and geodynamic processes. This study presents geological observations, zircon U-Pb ages, whole-rock major- and trace-element data, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopic analyses of the Early Cretaceous Laodaogou granitic complex in the SGXR. This complex, which hosts the Laodaogou Ag-Pb-Zn-(Sn) occurrence, consists of various types of plutonic rocks, including biotite monzogranites, biotite alkali-feldspar granites, alkali-feldspar granites, and porphyritic alkali-feldspar granitic dikes. Zircon U-Pb dating of these units yields Early Cretaceous ages ranging from ca. 145.3 Ma to 130.6 Ma. The four granite units exhibit broad variations in (K2O + Na2O)/CaO (5.63−56.64), FeOt/MgO (6.85−116.97), 10,000*Ga/Al (2.25−3.09), and Zr + Ce + Nb + Y contents (97.1−274.7 ppm). The elemental results coordinated U-Pb ages indicate that the Laodaogou complex exhibits high degrees of fractionation, evolving from I-type to A-type granites. The granite units show similar εHf(t) values (+7.7 to +16.1) and relatively young Hf model ages (650−165 Ma). It suggests that they were primarily derived from partial melting of a juvenile lower crust originating from a depleted mantle source. In the context of relevant published data, we propose that the retreating subduction of the Paleo-Pacific plate drove the Early Cretaceous magmatism in the SGXR. Asthenospheric mantle upwelling induced partial melting of the juvenile crust within the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, accompanied by magmatic differentiation and shallow emplacement. These processes culminated in regional Sn-Ag-Pb-Zn mineralization within the SGXR. Published: 2026-07-13T18:53:33+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.614 (must_read)

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38322.1

Relevance: 0.614 (must_read)

Mesozoic granitoids associated with Sn-Pb-Zn-Ag polymetallic deposits are prominent features in the southern Great Xing’an Range (SGXR), NE China. Understanding the origin and evolution of these granitoids is crucial for deciphering the associated mineralization and geodynamic processes. This study presents geological observations, zircon U-Pb ages, whole-rock major- and trace-element data, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopic analyses of the Early Cretaceous Laodaogou granitic complex in the SGXR. This complex, which hosts the Laodaogou Ag-Pb-Zn-(Sn) occurrence, consists of various types of plutonic rocks, including biotite monzogranites, biotite alkali-feldspar granites, alkali-feldspar granites, and porphyritic alkali-feldspar granitic dikes. Zircon U-Pb dating of these units yields Early Cretaceous ages ranging from ca. 145.3 Ma to 130.6 Ma. The four granite units exhibit broad variations in (K2O + Na2O)/CaO (5.63−56.64), FeOt/MgO (6.85−116.97), 10,000*Ga/Al (2.25−3.09), and Zr + Ce + Nb + Y contents (97.1−274.7 ppm). The elemental results coordinated U-Pb ages indicate that the Laodaogou complex exhibits high degrees of fractionation, evolving from I-type to A-type granites. The granite units show similar εHf(t) values (+7.7 to +16.1) and relatively young Hf model ages (650−165 Ma). It suggests that they were primarily derived from partial melting of a juvenile lower crust originating from a depleted mantle source. In the context of relevant published data, we propose that the retreating subduction of the Paleo-Pacific plate drove the Early Cretaceous magmatism in the SGXR. Asthenospheric mantle upwelling induced partial melting of the juvenile crust within the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, accompanied by magmatic differentiation and shallow emplacement. These processes culminated in regional Sn-Ag-Pb-Zn mineralization within the SGXR.

item.title: Evolution of Sulfur Concentration in the Deep Earth During the Proto‐Earth Growth: First‐Principles Study on the Sulfur Partitioning Between Metal and Silicate at High Pressure and Temperature
item.dc:creator: K. Itoh
T. Tsuchiya
item.description: Abstract Sulfur (S) is one of the promising light elements that can explain the density deficit of the core. The degree of siderophile nature of S under high pressure and temperature ( P , T ) is the key to estimating the S content in the core. However, a significant discrepancy between the metal‐silicate partition coefficient of S extrapolated from relatively low‐ P and that measured at deep mantle P , T is identified. In this study, we calculate the partition behavior of S between liquid iron and molten silicate in a wide range of P , T conditions using the ab initio thermodynamic integration molecular dynamics approach. The results indicate that S is strongly siderophile at 0–135 GPa and 3,000–5,000K, but the partition coefficient of S () markedly decreases with increasing the oxygen content in liquid iron. The pressure dependence of is also found to change around 20 GPa. These properties might reconcile the experimental conflicts and can be interpreted based on the atomic structures of the liquids. Using the derived , we model the evolution of the S concentrations in the core and mantle during the growth of the proto‐Earth. According to this model, the S concentration retained in the mantle increases as the size of the accreting planetesimals decreases and as the core becomes more oxidized. Our results further indicate that S could be a major light element in the core when the S concentration in the accreting planetesimals is larger than ∼3 mol%. Published: 2026-07-08T10:57:17+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.608 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: K. Itoh; T. Tsuchiya

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2026gc013030

Relevance: 0.608 (must_read)

Abstract Sulfur (S) is one of the promising light elements that can explain the density deficit of the core. The degree of siderophile nature of S under high pressure and temperature ( P , T ) is the key to estimating the S content in the core. However, a significant discrepancy between the metal‐silicate partition coefficient of S extrapolated from relatively low‐ P and that measured at deep mantle P , T is identified. In this study, we calculate the partition behavior of S between liquid iron and molten silicate in a wide range of P , T conditions using the ab initio thermodynamic integration molecular dynamics approach. The results indicate that S is strongly siderophile at 0–135 GPa and 3,000–5,000K, but the partition coefficient of S () markedly decreases with increasing the oxygen content in liquid iron. The pressure dependence of is also found to change around 20 GPa. These properties might reconcile the experimental conflicts and can be interpreted based on the atomic structures of the liquids. Using the derived , we model the evolution of the S concentrations in the core and mantle during the growth of the proto‐Earth. According to this model, the S concentration retained in the mantle increases as the size of the accreting planetesimals decreases and as the core becomes more oxidized. Our results further indicate that S could be a major light element in the core when the S concentration in the accreting planetesimals is larger than ∼3 mol%.

item.title: An Early Jurassic gold mineralization event in the Liaodong Peninsula, northeastern margin of the North China Craton: evidence from two types of magmatic and hydrothermal apatite in the Linjia deposit
item.dc:creator: Wanyi Feng
Jiahao Zheng
Bin Chen
Shuaijie Liu
item.description: Apatite is a widespread accessory mineral that occurs frequently in magmatic rocks and diverse hydrothermal ore deposits, and its textural features, geochronology, and geochemical signatures offer critical constraints on magmatic activity and metasomatic events. Here, two types of magmatic and hydrothermal apatite were identified by CL imaging in the hydrothermal altered auriferous granite in the Linjia gold deposit, Liaodong Peninsula, northeastern part of the North China Craton (NCC). The magmatic apatite (Ap1) grains with bright CL imaging formed earlier than the hydrothermal apatite (Ap2) crystals with dark CL imaging. The U-Pb dating results suggest the Ap1 formed contemporaneously with magmatic zircon in the granite at ca. 231 Ma, and these Triassic dates define the emplacement age of the ore-hosting granite. By contrast, the Ap2 and hydrothermal rutiles in the mineralized granite and schist both formed at ca. 186 Ma. The hydrothermal Ap2 grains show much higher S contents than the Ap1, suggesting they may be precipitated contemporaneously with sulfides from the S-bearing hydrothermal fluids. In addition, the calculated δ18Ofluid values, relatively high U as well as Th contents, and REE pattern of Ap2 are clearly distinct from metamorphic fluids, but comparable to magmatic‑hydrothermal fluids derived from granitic magmas. The textural, geochemical, and isotopic evidence indicates that Ap2 and rutiles formed from hydrothermal fluids during gold precipitation, and their Early Jurassic U-Pb ages represent the timing of gold mineralization. Based on the above evidence, we infer that Linjia represents an intrusion-related gold deposit related to a buried Early Jurassic felsic intrusion at depth. Importantly, the newly identified gold mineralization in the Linjia deposit coincides with the onset of paleo-Pacific plate subduction beneath the NCC, suggesting that there was a relatively small-scale Early Jurassic gold mineralization related to subduction prior to the ca. 120 Ma large-scale gold accumulation event in the NCC. This study highlights the importance of combining the microtextures, geochronological data, trace elements, and isotopic compositions of individual apatite grains to unravel the genesis of cryptic magmatic and hydrothermal apatite and reconstruct obscure geological events within magmatic-hydrothermal systems. Published: 2026-07-08T07:06:01+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.608 (must_read)

The U-Pb dating results suggest the Ap1 formed contemporaneously with magmatic zircon in the granite at ca. 231 Ma, and these Triassic dates define the emplacement age of the ore-hosting granite. By contrast, the Ap2 and hydrothermal rutiles in the mineralized granite and schist both formed at ca. 186 Ma. The hydrothermal Ap2 grains show much higher S contents than the Ap1, suggesting they may be precipitated contemporaneously with sulfides from the S-bearing hydrothermal fluids. The textural, geochemical, and isotopic evidence indicates that Ap2 and rutiles formed from hydrothermal fluids during gold precipitation, and their Early Jurassic U-Pb ages represent the timing of gold mineralization. Based on the above evidence, we infer that Linjia represents an intrusion-related gold deposit related to a buried Early Jurassic felsic intrusion at depth. Importantly, the newly identified gold mineralization in the Linjia deposit coincides with the onset of paleo-Pacific plate subduction beneath the NCC, suggesting that there was a relatively small-scale Early Jurassic gold mineralization related to subduction prior to the ca. 120 Ma large-scale gold accumulation event in the NCC. This study highlights the importance of combining the microtextures, geochronological data, trace elements, and isotopic compositions of individual apatite grains to unravel the genesis of cryptic magmatic and hydrothermal apatite and reconstruct obscure geological events within magmatic-hydrothermal systems.

item.title: Multiple mineralization events in the eastern Central Asian orogenic belt: Insights from geology, geochronology, and geochemistry of the giant Tongshan porphyry Cu deposit, NE China
item.dc:creator: Chenglin Bai
Guiqing Xie
Junkang Zhao
Tianqi Wang
Jie Chen
item.description: The Tongshan deposit is a giant porphyry Cu system in the eastern Central Asian orogenic belt where complex alteration and mineralization processes have remained poorly constrained. This study elucidated its evolution through integrated field geology, Re-Os geochronology, and sulfur isotope analysis. Mineralization in the western segment of Tongshan is hosted in granodiorite porphyry and tonalite and is associated with phyllic and localized potassic alteration. In the eastern segment, mineralization occurs within Ordovician Duobaoshan Formation volcanic rocks and the roof pendants of granodiorite and dacite porphyry, where propylitic alteration overprints earlier alteration assemblages. Two distinct mineralization episodes were identified: an initial porphyry-style mineralization (episode A) and a later overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization (episode B). Episode A comprises three stages (A-I, A-II, and A-III), with the main Cu mineralization in stage A-II forming quartz-chalcopyrite ± molybdenite ± pyrite veinlets with K-feldspar halos. Episode B veins overprinted earlier veins and contributed significant additional Cu through four stages (B-I, B-II, B-III, and B-IV) of wider quartz-sulfide veining. Systematic logging showed a positive correlation between the density of overprinting veins and Cu(-Mo-Au) grades. Molybdenite Re-Os dating suggests that episode A and B veins likely occurred at 475.6 ± 2.3 Ma and 228.1 ± 2.9 Ma, respectively. These ages suggest that the porphyry-style mineralization corresponds to the Ordovician granodiorite porphyry intrusions, whereas the overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization was associated with Triassic granodiorite and dacite porphyry. A significant characteristic at Tongshan is the prolonged interval of more than 200 m.y. between the two mineralization episodes. The δ34S values of the episode A sulfides range from −2.1‰ to −0.5‰, which is consistent with a magmatic source. The episode B sulfide δ34S signatures of −5.6‰ to +0.9‰ are analogous to those of a mantle-crust mixed source, indicating the Duobaoshan Formation may have been a potential sulfur reservoir. These results provide a refined genetic model for Tongshan and valuable insights for exploring other Paleozoic porphyry Cu deposits in the Central Asian orogenic belt. Published: 2026-07-09T21:55:56+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.596 (must_read)

The Tongshan deposit is a giant porphyry Cu system in the eastern Central Asian orogenic belt where complex alteration and mineralization processes have remained poorly constrained. This study elucidated its evolution through integrated field geology, Re-Os geochronology, and sulfur isotope analysis. Mineralization in the western segment of Tongshan is hosted in granodiorite porphyry and tonalite and is associated with phyllic and localized potassic alteration. In the eastern segment, mineralization occurs within Ordovician Duobaoshan Formation volcanic rocks and the roof pendants of granodiorite and dacite porphyry, where propylitic alteration overprints earlier alteration assemblages. Two distinct mineralization episodes were identified: an initial porphyry-style mineralization (episode A) and a later overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization (episode B). Episode A comprises three stages (A-I, A-II, and A-III), with the main Cu mineralization in stage A-II forming quartz-chalcopyrite ± molybdenite ± pyrite veinlets with K-feldspar halos. Episode B veins overprinted earlier veins and contributed significant additional Cu through four stages (B-I, B-II, B-III, and B-IV) of wider quartz-sulfide veining. Systematic logging showed a positive correlation between the density of overprinting veins and Cu(-Mo-Au) grades. Molybdenite Re-Os dating suggests that episode A and B veins likely occurred at 475.6 ± 2.3 Ma and 228.1 ± 2.9 Ma, respectively. These ages suggest that the porphyry-style mineralization corresponds to the Ordovician granodiorite porphyry intrusions, whereas the overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization was associated with Triassic granodiorite and dacite porphyry. A significant characteristic at Tongshan is the prolonged interval of more than 200 m.y. between the two mineralization episodes. The δ34S values of the episode A sulfides range from −2.1‰ to −0.5‰, which is consistent with a magmatic source. The episode B sulfide δ34S signatures of −5.6‰ to +0.9‰ are analogous to those of a mantle-crust mixed source, indicating the Duobaoshan Formation may have been a potential sulfur reservoir. These results provide a refined genetic model for Tongshan and valuable insights for exploring other Paleozoic porphyry Cu deposits in the Central Asian orogenic belt.

item.title: Sulfur and lead isotope geochemistry of sulfide minerals from the Yushui copper deposit, eastern Guangdong, South China
item.dc:creator: Gang Chen
Changhui Ke
Junzeng Zuo
Zhihui Dai
Maohong Chen
item.description: The Yushui volcanogenic massive sulfide (VMS) copper deposit in eastern Guangdong, South China, is distinguished by two discrete mineralization events: a Carboniferous stratiform ore system (ca. 308 Ma) and a Jurassic vein-type overprint (ca. 163 Ma). In situ sulfur and lead isotope analyses (LA‑MC‑ICP‑MS) were conducted on sulfides from both ore types to constrain sulfur and metal sources and hydrothermal system evolution. Sulfur isotopes (δ34S = − 23.88‰ to + 4.01‰) show systematic variations: early exhalative and stockwork ores record significant bacterial and thermochemical reduction of seawater sulfate, whereas main‑stage massive and banded ores approach magmatic sulfur values (0‰ to + 3‰). Vein‑type ores yield δ34S indicative of a dominant magmatic source linked to Jurassic igneous activity. Lead isotopes form three linear groups corresponding to Carboniferous VMS mineralization, Jurassic vein‑type overprint, and host volcanic rocks. Lead in stratiform ores was largely leached from basement crust with minor magmatic input, while vein‑type ores predominantly inherited lead from pre‑existing stratiform ores in a closed system. These results confirm a two‑stage genesis: (1) shallow‑marine seafloor hydrothermal sedimentation forming primary VMS ore bodies at ∼ 308 Ma; (2) Jurassic magmatic‑hydrothermal reworking and superimposition generating vein‑type ore bodies at ∼ 163 Ma. This study highlights the utility of in situ S–Pb isotopes in unravelling polyphase ore‑forming processes in VMS deposits modified by later tectono‑magmatic events. Published: 2026-07-08T07:06:10+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.596 (consider)
