In situ sulfur and lead isotope analyses (LA‑MC‑ICP‑MS) were conducted on sulfides from both ore types to constrain sulfur and metal sources and hydrothermal system evolution. Sulfur isotopes (δ34S = − 23.88‰ to + 4.01‰) show systematic variations: early exhalative and stockwork ores record significant bacterial and thermochemical reduction of seawater sulfate, whereas main‑stage massive and banded ores approach magmatic sulfur values (0‰ to + 3‰). Vein‑type ores yield δ34S indicative of a dominant magmatic source linked to Jurassic igneous activity. Lead isotopes form three linear groups corresponding to Carboniferous VMS mineralization, Jurassic vein‑type overprint, and host volcanic rocks. Lead in stratiform ores was largely leached from basement crust with minor magmatic input, while vein‑type ores predominantly inherited lead from pre‑existing stratiform ores in a closed system. These results confirm a two‑stage genesis: (1) shallow‑marine seafloor hydrothermal sedimentation forming primary VMS ore bodies at ∼ 308 Ma; (2) Jurassic magmatic‑hydrothermal reworking and superimposition generating vein‑type ore bodies at ∼ 163 Ma. This study highlights the utility of in situ S–Pb isotopes in unravelling polyphase ore‑forming processes in VMS deposits modified by later tectono‑magmatic events.

item.title: Crustal growth and reworking of the Southern West African craton: Implications for regional tectonics
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Thébaud
Quentin Masurel
Luis Para-Avila
Aurélien Eglinger
Julien Perret
Augustin Yao Koffi
Ousmane Wane
Helen McFarlane
Graham Begg
Guillaume Duclaux
Mark Jessell
Olivier Vanderhaeghe
Lenka Baratoux
Mahamadou Diallo
Alain Kouamelan
Tony Kemp
Noreen J. Evans
Armin Zeh
item.description: This contribution reviews the crustal growth of the West African Craton and its geodynamic evolution using whole rock geochemistry together with U-Pb geochronological and Lu-Hf isotopic records preserved in igneous zircon grains from felsic plutonic rocks. Our study shows that the crustal growth in the southern West African Craton during the late Siderian and most of the Rhyacian (~2350-2300 Ma and ~2300-2060 Ma, respectively) was largely driven by the opening and subsequent closure of a large oceanic basin that fragmented the Neoarchean continental lithosphere. This tectonic evolution involved two major orogenic cycles including the Eoeburnean (~2285-2120 Ma) and Eburnean (~2120-2095 Ma) orogenies, each marked by distinct pulses of both juvenile magmatism and associated crustal reworking. Geochemical, geochronological, and isotopic data acquired on Eoeburnean plutonic rocks are used to investigate the process leading to crustal growth and its evolution during the Rhyacian. In central and western Ghana, central Cote d’Ivoire, and Burkina Faso, melt derivation from sources where hornblende and/or garnet were stable is consistent with crustal thickening and the injection of mantle derived magmas. In contrast, eastern and southeastern portions of the sWAC record reworking of Archean crust, as indicated by negative ƐHf values, whereas the western Kénéma-Man Domain over in Liberia and Sierra Leone exhibit intact Archean continental crust. During the Eburnean orogeny, more extensive crustal melting occurred at both deep and shallow structural levels, with ƐHf values indicating both continued juvenile input and reworking of earlier crust, including ongoing reworking of the northern margin of the Archean Kénéma-Man Domain. Late-orogenic bimodal magmatism (~2095-2040 Ma) in southern Guinea is interpreted as a response to asthenospheric upwelling, likely triggered by lithospheric delamination or slab break-off following arc-continent collision. These magmatic rocks exhibit mixed isotopic signatures, consistent with contributions from both mantle-derived and reworked crustal sources. We propose that the tectonic evolution of the southern West African Craton was controlled by at least two partly synchronous subduction zone segments. The first was active during the Eoeburnean orogenic cycle and was southeast-dipping beneath the Archean BurGhana block in the eastern part of the craton. The second one was active during the Eburnean orogenic cycle and east-dipping, likely extending northwards into the Reguibat Domain and extended in the southern West African Craton where Precambrian terrains accreted onto the Archean Kénéma-Man Domain. The convergence and diachronous collision of intra-oceanic arcs with Archean lithospheric blocks (Kénéma-Man, BurGhana, Reguibat) marked the protracted closure of a ~3,500 km wide oceanic basin. Published: 2026-07-13T05:52:38+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.595 (consider)

Evans; Armin Zeh

This contribution reviews the crustal growth of the West African Craton and its geodynamic evolution using whole rock geochemistry together with U-Pb geochronological and Lu-Hf isotopic records preserved in igneous zircon grains from felsic plutonic rocks. Our study shows that the crustal growth in the southern West African Craton during the late Siderian and most of the Rhyacian (~2350-2300 Ma and ~2300-2060 Ma, respectively) was largely driven by the opening and subsequent closure of a large oceanic basin that fragmented the Neoarchean continental lithosphere. Geochemical, geochronological, and isotopic data acquired on Eoeburnean plutonic rocks are used to investigate the process leading to crustal growth and its evolution during the Rhyacian. In central and western Ghana, central Cote d’Ivoire, and Burkina Faso, melt derivation from sources where hornblende and/or garnet were stable is consistent with crustal thickening and the injection of mantle derived magmas. In contrast, eastern and southeastern portions of the sWAC record reworking of Archean crust, as indicated by negative ƐHf values, whereas the western Kénéma-Man Domain over in Liberia and Sierra Leone exhibit intact Archean continental crust. During the Eburnean orogeny, more extensive crustal melting occurred at both deep and shallow structural levels, with ƐHf values indicating both continued juvenile input and reworking of earlier crust, including ongoing reworking of the northern margin of the Archean Kénéma-Man Domain. Late-orogenic bimodal magmatism (~2095-2040 Ma) in southern Guinea is interpreted as a response to asthenospheric upwelling, likely triggered by lithospheric delamination or slab break-off following arc-continent collision. These magmatic rocks exhibit mixed isotopic signatures, consistent with contributions from both mantle-derived and reworked crustal sources. We propose that the tectonic evolution of the southern West African Craton was controlled by at least two partly synchronous subduction zone segments. The first was active during the Eoeburnean orogenic cycle and was southeast-dipping beneath the Archean BurGhana block in the eastern part of the craton. The second one was active during the Eburnean orogenic cycle and east-dipping, likely extending northwards into the Reguibat Domain and extended in the southern West African Craton where Precambrian terrains accreted onto the Archean Kénéma-Man Domain. The convergence and diachronous collision of intra-oceanic arcs with Archean lithospheric blocks (Kénéma-Man, BurGhana, Reguibat) marked the protracted closure of a ~3,500 km wide oceanic basin.

item.title: Paleoproterozoic orogenic gold system in the Contendas-Mirante belt, São Francisco Craton, Brazil: Insights from lithogeochemistry, U–Pb detrital zircon geochronology, and geodynamic analysis
item.dc:creator: Ricardo Ramos Spreafico
Astrid Holzheid
Elson Paiva Oliveira
Carlos Marcello Dias Fernandes
Carlos Eduardo Ganade de Araujo
Fabiano Cruz do Livramento
Antônio Marcos Vitória de Moraes
item.description: Orogenic gold systems are important sources of Paleoproterozoic gold mineralization in cratonic settings worldwide and are commonly associated with convergent-margin tectonics and crustal-scale fluid flow. In the northern São Francisco Craton, eastern Brazil, several Rhyacian-Orosirian gold occurrences are known; however, the geodynamic context and structural framework controlling mineralization in the Contendas-Mirante volcanosedimentary belt remain poorly constrained. This study integrates geological and structural mapping, core sample analysis, whole-rock geochemistry, U–Pb detrital zircon geochronology, and aerogeophysical data to constrain the tectonic setting and mineralization processes of the Contendas-Mirante belt. Metabasalts of the Neoarchean lower unit display light rare earth element-enriched patterns, elevated Th and U contents, and pronounced negative Nb–Ta–Ti anomalies, consistent with a continental geochemical signature. Elevated Th/Yb, La/Yb, and Th/Nb ratios may have been partly modified by secondary processes and are therefore considered supportive, but not restricted to an arc-like continental geochemical signature, as they may also reflect crustal contamination during the evolution of basaltic magma. U–Pb detrital zircon ages from associated phyllites yield a maximum depositional age of 2626 ± 31 Ma, indicating provenance from proximal Neoarchean volcanic sources along a continental margin. Structural analyses reveal a dominant north–south-trending crustal-scale lineament formed during the Rhyacian-Orosirian tectonothermal event, itself associated with the accretion of oceanic and continental arcs and older continental blocks in the northern São Francisco Craton. Subordinate northwest-southeast and northeast-southwest structures controlled the distribution of lithologies and fluid migration. Gold mineralization in the Contendas-Mirante belt is hosted by brecciated ferruginous metacherts of the lower unit and is spatially associated with quartz-sericite schists, reflecting fluid focusing within brittle-ductile deformation zones where iron-rich sedimentary rocks acted as effective chemical and rheological traps. The results support a tectonic evolution involving Neoarchean continental-margin volcanism, followed by the Rhyacian-Orosirian tectonothermal event and orogenic gold mineralization, most likely within a continental arc system. The Contendas-Mirante belt mineralization exhibits key temporal, structural, and geodynamic characteristics of Paleoproterozoic orogenic gold systems in the Congo, Amazonian, and West African cratons, contributing to broader reconstructions of crustal growth, metallogenesis, and gold exploration models in the São Francisco Craton and analogous cratonic provinces worldwide. Orogenic gold systems are important sources of Paleoproterozoic gold mineralization in cratonic settings worldwide and are commonly associated with convergent-margin tectonics and crustal-scale fluid flow. The results support a tectonic evolution involving Neoarchean continental-margin volcanism, followed by the Rhyacian-Orosirian tectonothermal event and orogenic gold mineralization, most likely within a continental arc system. item.title: Differential exhumation of giant lode gold deposits in the Jiaodong Peninsula, eastern China
item.dc:creator: Hongtao Zhao
Yu Zhang
Lin Gong
Martin Danišík
Yongjun Shao
Yuanming Pan
Yanbo Xu
Teimoor Nazari-Dehkordi
Yuzhou Feng
item.description: The Jiaodong Peninsula is China’s premier gold province, containing >5500 t of gold reserves, and exhibits pronounced spatial variation in lode gold mineralization. This variation is especially evident along the Jiaojia fault zone, where the northern segment hosts >90% of the gold endowment, while the southern segment is only sparsely mineralized. To understand this pattern, we integrate new apatite (U-Th)/He (AHe; n = 48 single-grain ages from eight samples, 50.1 ± 9.6 Ma to 2.8 ± 0.7 Ma) and zircon (U-Th)/He (ZHe; n = 40 single-grain ages from eight samples, 133.8 ± 6.1 Ma to 106.8 ± 5.0 Ma) ages obtained from a vertical drill-core profile (Dayinjia ZK32-1; surface to −1492 m depth) in the southern Jiaojia fault zone with a comprehensive low-temperature thermochronologic database (70 zircon fission-track [ZFT], 164 apatite fission-track [AFT], 84 ZHe, and 52 AHe). Thermal modeling reveals five distinct exhumation phases (120−95 Ma, 95−60 Ma, 60−45 Ma, 45−15 Ma, and 15 Ma to present) occurring in the southern segment. Regional analysis reveals substantially less post-mineralization exhumation along the southern Jiaojia fault zone relative to the northern segment. This disparity is likely responsible for the lower gold endowment found in the south. Regionally, two major post-mineralization exhumation episodes (110−70 Ma and 50−30 Ma) in the Jiaodong Peninsula reflect tectonic transitions driven by evolving subduction dynamics. These episodes indicate elevated exhumation rates and greater cumulative denudation in the eastern peninsula. This likely limited ore preservation, contributing to the heterogeneous distribution of gold resources across the peninsula. Notably, the older thermochronologic ages at the low elevations of key gold districts, such as Sanshandao, Jiaojia, and Linglong, suggest limited post-mineralization exhumation, which has contributed to the preservation of these ore deposits. These findings underscore the critical relationship between exhumation processes and the spatial variation in gold mineralization and demonstrate that low-temperature thermochronology is an effective tool for guiding the exploration of deep-lying lode gold deposits. Published: 2026-07-10T20:14:07+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.595 (consider)

These episodes indicate elevated exhumation rates and greater cumulative denudation in the eastern peninsula. item.title: Natural CO2 sequestration: mechanisms and conditions of carbonate precipitation in basalt
item.dc:creator: M.P. Rosenqvist
K.G. Dunkel
L.P. Nogueira
S. Planke
S. Polteau
M. Ardit
F. Nestola
L. Menegon
item.description: Carbon sequestration in mafic and ultramafic rocks has been demonstrated as a safe and efficient storage method; however, the specifics of the carbonation reactions remain uncertain, especially in volcanic reservoirs. In this study, we contribute to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms and conditions of natural CO2 sequestration in basalt by analysing 43 early Eocene basalt samples from IODP boreholes U1571A and U1572A on the Skoll High on the mid-Norwegian continental margin. Using high-resolution 2D and 3D imaging and microanalytical techniques together with X-ray powder diffraction, U-Pb dating, as well as carbon and oxygen isotope analysis, we identified two main types of carbonate precipitation: 1) direct precipitation in vesicles, and 2) replacement of olivine and clinopyroxene by coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. While most vesicles are coated with smectite, this did not inhibit fluid flow and carbonate precipitation. Mineral parageneses, δ13C ratios, and δ18O ratios record carbonate precipitation in the vesicles at < 100˚C and suggest a variation of carbon sources, from early intruded meteoric water mixed with volcanic and thermogenic carbon to marine sourced carbon. Vesicle carbonate comprises up to 8.6 vol% of its host basalt (as determined by μ-CT image analysis) and replacement carbonate 0.6 – 0.94 wt% (as determined by powder diffraction). We estimate that within the upper 100 m of the subaerially emplaced basalt sequence on Skoll High, approximately 1 Mt of CO2 is stored as carbonate per km2 area, at least 0.3 Mt of which has precipitated in the microporous basalt through coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. This illustrates that the storage potential of basaltic rocks is not limited to the macro-porosity (vesicles) but may be significantly enhanced by replacement reactions. Published: 2026-07-09T12:39:23+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.594 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: M.P. Rosenqvist; K.G. Dunkel; L.P. Nogueira; S. Planke; S. Polteau; M. Ardit; F. Nestola; L. Menegon

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.07.013

Relevance: 0.594 (consider)

Carbon sequestration in mafic and ultramafic rocks has been demonstrated as a safe and efficient storage method; however, the specifics of the carbonation reactions remain uncertain, especially in volcanic reservoirs. In this study, we contribute to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms and conditions of natural CO2 sequestration in basalt by analysing 43 early Eocene basalt samples from IODP boreholes U1571A and U1572A on the Skoll High on the mid-Norwegian continental margin. Using high-resolution 2D and 3D imaging and microanalytical techniques together with X-ray powder diffraction, U-Pb dating, as well as carbon and oxygen isotope analysis, we identified two main types of carbonate precipitation: 1) direct precipitation in vesicles, and 2) replacement of olivine and clinopyroxene by coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. While most vesicles are coated with smectite, this did not inhibit fluid flow and carbonate precipitation. Mineral parageneses, δ13C ratios, and δ18O ratios record carbonate precipitation in the vesicles at < 100˚C and suggest a variation of carbon sources, from early intruded meteoric water mixed with volcanic and thermogenic carbon to marine sourced carbon. Vesicle carbonate comprises up to 8.6 vol% of its host basalt (as determined by μ-CT image analysis) and replacement carbonate 0.6 – 0.94 wt% (as determined by powder diffraction). We estimate that within the upper 100 m of the subaerially emplaced basalt sequence on Skoll High, approximately 1 Mt of CO2 is stored as carbonate per km2 area, at least 0.3 Mt of which has precipitated in the microporous basalt through coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. This illustrates that the storage potential of basaltic rocks is not limited to the macro-porosity (vesicles) but may be significantly enhanced by replacement reactions.

item.title: Repeated recharge of primitive melts in the lower oceanic crust: Constraints from the Dongco ophiolite, Central Tibet
item.dc:creator: Xiao-Ni Li
Chuan-Zhou Liu
Wei-Qi Zhang
Tong Liu
Chang Zhang
Yin-Zheng Lin
Zhen Zhang
item.description: Magma recharge and associated melt–mush interaction can substantially modify the composition of the lower oceanic crust. However, the mineralogical and geochemical characteristics of such interaction remain poorly constrained. Here, we present petrological, geochronological, and geochemical data for poikilitic olivine gabbros, layered olivine gabbros and layered gabbros from the Dongco ophiolite in central Tibet. Zircon U-Pb ages and mineral Sr-Nd isotopes indicate that the poikilitic olivine gabbros and their host layered gabbros were coeval and derived from the same magmatic system. Both the poikilitic olivine gabbros and the layered olivine gabbros record evidence of melt–mush interaction. The poikilitic olivine gabbros display clear disequilibrium textures, including clinopyroxene oikocrysts enclosing plagioclase chadacrysts and vermicular orthopyroxene surrounding olivine grains. The clinopyroxene oikocrysts exhibit oscillatory and normal Cr zoning from core to rim. In contrast, the layered olivine gabbros lack conspicuous reaction textures, whereas clinopyroxene in these rocks preserves oscillatory, normal, and reverse Cr zoning, indicating cryptic melt–mush interaction. These textural and mineral-chemical features indicate that both the poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros recorded melt–mush interaction, but at different levels of textural expression. In addition, clinopyroxene grains from both poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros are characterized by low TiO2 contents and the absence of significant core-to-rim variations in incompatible trace elements, suggesting that the reacting melt was primitive rather than evolved. Assimilation-fractionation-crystallization modelling further indicates that clinopyroxene oikocrysts in the poikilitic olivine gabbros formed under melt-dominated reaction conditions, with limited assimilation of pre-existing mush components, consistent with their coarse grain sizes. In contrast, clinopyroxene in the layered olivine gabbros records a larger contribution from assimilated or reacted mush components relative to crystallization, indicating stronger mush-buffered modification. These results demonstrate that repeated recharge of primitive melts can modify mineral compositions and geochemical signatures of gabbroic cumulates, thereby contributing to the chemical evolution of the lower oceanic crust. Published: 2026-07-09T19:58:13+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.588 (consider)

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108709

Relevance: 0.588 (consider)

Magma recharge and associated melt–mush interaction can substantially modify the composition of the lower oceanic crust. However, the mineralogical and geochemical characteristics of such interaction remain poorly constrained. Here, we present petrological, geochronological, and geochemical data for poikilitic olivine gabbros, layered olivine gabbros and layered gabbros from the Dongco ophiolite in central Tibet. Zircon U-Pb ages and mineral Sr-Nd isotopes indicate that the poikilitic olivine gabbros and their host layered gabbros were coeval and derived from the same magmatic system. Both the poikilitic olivine gabbros and the layered olivine gabbros record evidence of melt–mush interaction. The poikilitic olivine gabbros display clear disequilibrium textures, including clinopyroxene oikocrysts enclosing plagioclase chadacrysts and vermicular orthopyroxene surrounding olivine grains. The clinopyroxene oikocrysts exhibit oscillatory and normal Cr zoning from core to rim. In contrast, the layered olivine gabbros lack conspicuous reaction textures, whereas clinopyroxene in these rocks preserves oscillatory, normal, and reverse Cr zoning, indicating cryptic melt–mush interaction. These textural and mineral-chemical features indicate that both the poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros recorded melt–mush interaction, but at different levels of textural expression. In addition, clinopyroxene grains from both poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros are characterized by low TiO2 contents and the absence of significant core-to-rim variations in incompatible trace elements, suggesting that the reacting melt was primitive rather than evolved. Assimilation-fractionation-crystallization modelling further indicates that clinopyroxene oikocrysts in the poikilitic olivine gabbros formed under melt-dominated reaction conditions, with limited assimilation of pre-existing mush components, consistent with their coarse grain sizes. In contrast, clinopyroxene in the layered olivine gabbros records a larger contribution from assimilated or reacted mush components relative to crystallization, indicating stronger mush-buffered modification. These results demonstrate that repeated recharge of primitive melts can modify mineral compositions and geochemical signatures of gabbroic cumulates, thereby contributing to the chemical evolution of the lower oceanic crust.

item.title: Geotectonic−metallogenic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys in the northern Tibetan Plateau: A synthesis from divergent to convergent settings
item.dc:creator: Wenyuan Li
Bing Qian
Matthew J. Brzozowski
Minxin You
Lu Li
item.description: The tectonic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys Ocean and its associated metallogenic processes in the northern Tibetan Plateau are pivotal topics within the field of Earth sciences. Despite recent advances, a detailed understanding of its evolutionary history and metallogenic mechanisms remains elusive. This contribution, grounded in the metallogenic characteristics of the northern Tibetan Plateau from the late Paleozoic to early Mesozoic, provides a systematic dissection of the tectonic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys and its profound impact on the metallogenesis of this region. The study identifies two branches of the Paleo-Tethys. The northern branch, situated between the Kangxiwa−Animaqing−Mianlue suture zone and the Xijingwulan−Jinshajiang−Ganzi−Litang suture zone, represents the main ocean basin, whereas the southern branch, situated within the Longmu Co−Shuanghu suture zone between the Northern and Southern Qiangtang terranes, formed through post-arc expansion. This contribution not only clarifies the timing of initiation (Early Devonian) and closure (Late Triassic) of the Paleo-Tethys but also categorizes its metallogenic processes into two major types based on tectonic cycles—those associated with divergent settings and those associated with convergent settings. Metallogenesis associated with divergent settings is exemplified by the super-large Xiarihamu magmatic Ni-Cu-Co sulfide deposit, underscoring the role of Paleo-Tethys rifting in this ocean basin’s metallogenic evolution. In contrast, metallogenesis associated with convergent settings is characterized by numerous medium- to small-scale late Permian to Triassic porphyry−skarn−polymetallic deposits and pegmatite-hosted Li-Be deposits, highlighting the significant contributions of oceanic subduction and collisional orogeny to mineralization. The discovery of Triassic super-large orogenic Au deposits in the Eastern Kunlun and Western Qinling orogens, as well as the pegmatite-hosted Li deposits at Dahongliutan and Jiajika, located in the Western Kunlun orogen and Songpan−Ganzi orogen, respectively, further supports the intimate link between the tectonic and metallogenic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys. Published: 2026-07-13T18:53:32+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.581 (consider)

In contrast, metallogenesis associated with convergent settings is characterized by numerous medium- to small-scale late Permian to Triassic porphyry−skarn−polymetallic deposits and pegmatite-hosted Li-Be deposits, highlighting the significant contributions of oceanic subduction and collisional orogeny to mineralization. The discovery of Triassic super-large orogenic Au deposits in the Eastern Kunlun and Western Qinling orogens, as well as the pegmatite-hosted Li deposits at Dahongliutan and Jiajika, located in the Western Kunlun orogen and Songpan−Ganzi orogen, respectively, further supports the intimate link between the tectonic and metallogenic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys.

item.title: Structure, composition and dynamics of divergent and convergent plate margins
item.dc:creator: Yong-Fei Zheng
Yi-Xiang Chen
Li-Qun Dai
Wei Dan
Sanzhong Li
item.description: Plate margins are the dynamic boundaries where lithospheric plates interact, driving Earth’s tectonic evolution. While the asthenospheric material and energy are transported along divergent plate margins to the Earth’s surface, the surface material is transferred along convergent plate margins into the Earth’s interior. Therefore, plate divergence and convergence are two key mechanisms for the exchange of material and energy between the lithosphere and asthenosphere. A great deal of advances in understanding thestructure, composition and dynamics of divergent and convergent plate margins have been made in the past two decades. Divergent margins are manifested as seafloor spreading and continental rift zones, and they occur as mid-ocean ridges, successful and failed continental rifts in different phases. Convergent margins are manifested as oceanic subduction and continental collision zones, and their formation and evolution are dictated by the properties of converging plates. Therefore, they record a series of processes for the Wilson cycle in plate tectonics. The thermobaric and dynamic regimes of plate margins are two critical variables in modulating the internal and external Earth systems. In general, the geothermal gradient of plate margins changes from cold through warm to hot during the transition from convergence to divergence, and it correlates with the dynamic regime of either horizontal or vertical forces via specific tectonic processes. Cold subduction proceeds at low geothermal gradients, producing low T/P Alpine-type blueschist to eclogite facies metamorphic rocks; warm collision proceeds at mediate geothermal gradients, leading to medium T/P Barrovian-type amphibolite to granulite facies metamorphic rocks; and hot rifting proceeds at high geothermal gradients, generating high T/P amphibolite to granulite facies metamorphic rocks. Oceanic plates may subduct either actively or passively beneath continental margins, giving rise to different products at the overriding continental margins. Active subduction results in accretionary wedge, andesitic arc magmatism and porphyry mineralization, which are absent in the case of passive subduction. Continental assembly progresses from soft collision through hard collision to deep subduction, producing different types of regional metamorphism along convergent plate margins. Syn-collisional orogeny during continental assembly is characterized by medium T/P Barrovian-type metamorphism in the compressional regime, whereas post-collisional orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. Such spatiotemporal relationships between the dynamic and thermobaric regimes are definite at plate margins. Magmatism always proceeds at high geothermal gradients and thus is associated with extensional regimes either in the late stage of oceanic subduction or in the post-collisional stage. In this regard, caution must be exercised in linking HT/LP metamorphism and felsic magmatism to continental collision and supercontinental assembly. The formation and evolution of intracontinental tectonics have the inheritance and development relationships to the structure and composition of precursor plate margins. The thermobaric and dynamic regimes are two key variables in dictating the physicochemical properties and geodynamic processes of continental interiors. Thethermo-mechanical coupling is significant at the plate margins, where feedback between thermobaric regimes (i.e., geothermal gradients) and dynamic forces (i.e., buoyancy vs gravity, and compression vs extension) governs a series of geological processes such as deformation, metamorphism and magmatism. Therefore, plate tectonics operate viafeedback loopswhere thermobaric anomalies (e.g., asthenospheric heating vs slab cooling) modify rock rheology, inducing these geological processes that reshapes the architecture of active and fossil plate margins. As a result, continental rifting along previously convergent plate margins plays a critical role not only in physical and chemical reworking of the plate margins but also in initiating new Wilson cycles. As such, intracontinental orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. The relevant elements are the important components of plate tectonics theory and thus have significant linkages to resources, energy and disasters. Published: 2026-07-09T12:55:31+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.579 (consider)
item.content:encoded:

While the asthenospheric material and energy are transported along divergent plate margins to the Earth’s surface, the surface material is transferred along convergent plate margins into the Earth’s interior. Therefore, they record a series of processes for the Wilson cycle in plate tectonics. Syn-collisional orogeny during continental assembly is characterized by medium T/P Barrovian-type metamorphism in the compressional regime, whereas post-collisional orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. Thethermo-mechanical coupling is significant at the plate margins, where feedback between thermobaric regimes (i.e., geothermal gradients) and dynamic forces (i.e., buoyancy vs gravity, and compression vs extension) governs a series of geological processes such as deformation, metamorphism and magmatism. Therefore, plate tectonics operate viafeedback loopswhere thermobaric anomalies (e.g., asthenospheric heating vs slab cooling) modify rock rheology, inducing these geological processes that reshapes the architecture of active and fossil plate margins. As a result, continental rifting along previously convergent plate margins plays a critical role not only in physical and chemical reworking of the plate margins but also in initiating new Wilson cycles. As such, intracontinental orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. The relevant elements are the important components of plate tectonics theory and thus have significant linkages to resources, energy and disasters.

item.title: Time series analysis of mantle cycles, Part III: The 90 Myr cycle in the frequency of igneous zircon ages
item.dc:creator: Kent C. Condie
Stephen J. Puetz
item.description: A 90 Myr quasi-periodic cycle in zircon ages from igneous rocks has persisted for at least 3500 Myr. Although some age peaks from the 90 Myr cycle may correspond to the supercontinent cycle, there is no obvious relationship with the assembly or breakup phases. Our time-series of the globality of igneous activity shows that even the largest igneous events comprise less than 36% of the continental crust by volume. This implies that igneous events are regional rather than global in extent. The globality estimates of large igneous provinces are also rather small, in the range of 5%–15%, having a far smaller sample size than that from the igneous database. Another time-series shows that peaks in surface area of orogenic activity fluctuate proportionally to variation in igneous activity. This correlation indicates that the tectonic processes that trigger orogenic activity are closely associated with global variation in igneous activity. A dominant mantle event at 2700 Ma resulted in a rapid increase in the orogen/terrane area ratio, perhaps reflecting initiation of widespread plate tectonics. Considering the collective results from all time-series, the tectonic activity associated with the 90 Myr cycle appears to be regional and in any case not global. Both collisional and accretionary phases of orogens are characterized by mixed ε Hf(t) and ε Nd(t) values, positive in some instances and negative in others, with no discernable pattern. The large troughs in ε Nd(t) values at 2000–1800 Ma and 600–350 Ma correlate with the assembly of supercontinents Columbia and Gondwana-Pangea, respectively, and peaks in ε Nd(t) with positive signatures (1400, 1200, 800, and 700 Ma) correspond to the breakup of these two supercontinents. Although some of the peaks from the 90 Myr model have positive ε Hf(t) and ε Nd(t) and negative δ 18O suggesting juvenile crustal additions, they do not require net growth of juvenile crust because other peaks that align with this cycle have crustal reworking signatures. We speculate that the 90 Myr cycle is linked to periodic mantle magma generation caused by thermal instabilities in the deep mantle. This process could be reinforced by a related cycle in mantle plumes. It is also possible that some episodes of subduction, slab avalanches, and other upstairs-driven mantle events are at least partly controlled by an already existing 90 Myr cycle produced by these thermal instabilities in the deep mantle. Published: 2026-07-13T06:34:47+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.577 (consider)

A 90 Myr quasi-periodic cycle in zircon ages from igneous rocks has persisted for at least 3500 Myr. Although some age peaks from the 90 Myr cycle may correspond to the supercontinent cycle, there is no obvious relationship with the assembly or breakup phases. Our time-series of the globality of igneous activity shows that even the largest igneous events comprise less than 36% of the continental crust by volume. This implies that igneous events are regional rather than global in extent. The globality estimates of large igneous provinces are also rather small, in the range of 5%–15%, having a far smaller sample size than that from the igneous database. Another time-series shows that peaks in surface area of orogenic activity fluctuate proportionally to variation in igneous activity. This correlation indicates that the tectonic processes that trigger orogenic activity are closely associated with global variation in igneous activity. A dominant mantle event at 2700 Ma resulted in a rapid increase in the orogen/terrane area ratio, perhaps reflecting initiation of widespread plate tectonics. Considering the collective results from all time-series, the tectonic activity associated with the 90 Myr cycle appears to be regional and in any case not global. Both collisional and accretionary phases of orogens are characterized by mixed ε Hf(t) and ε Nd(t) values, positive in some instances and negative in others, with no discernable pattern. The large troughs in ε Nd(t) values at 2000–1800 Ma and 600–350 Ma correlate with the assembly of supercontinents Columbia and Gondwana-Pangea, respectively, and peaks in ε Nd(t) with positive signatures (1400, 1200, 800, and 700 Ma) correspond to the breakup of these two supercontinents. Although some of the peaks from the 90 Myr model have positive ε Hf(t) and ε Nd(t) and negative δ 18O suggesting juvenile crustal additions, they do not require net growth of juvenile crust because other peaks that align with this cycle have crustal reworking signatures. We speculate that the 90 Myr cycle is linked to periodic mantle magma generation caused by thermal instabilities in the deep mantle. It is also possible that some episodes of subduction, slab avalanches, and other upstairs-driven mantle events are at least partly controlled by an already existing 90 Myr cycle produced by these thermal instabilities in the deep mantle.

item.title: Sequential serpentinization reactions revealed by boron isotope systematics of the Lost City vent fluids
item.dc:creator: Aled D. Evans
Heather J. Goring-Harford
Rosalind M. Coggon
Rachael H. James
Caitlin McCarthy
Matthew J. Cooper
Gavin L. Foster
Gretchen L. Früh-Green
Susan Q. Lang
Damon A.H. Teagle
item.description: Hydrothermal vent fluids reflect interactions between oceanic lithosphere and seawater, but to date, the processes resulting in unusual hyperalkaline (up to pH 11) fluids venting ultramafic-hosted systems, such as the Lost City Hydrothermal Field, remain enigmatic. To elucidate fluid reaction pathways, we present boron (B) concentration and isotope analyses from fluids venting the Lost City, located off axis atop the serpentinized Atlantis Massif just west of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge at 30°N. Our data reveal that fluid B concentrations are markedly lower and isotopically lighter than that of seawater. These data contrast with the accepted understanding of B isotope systematics that predicts that the progressive removal of B from seawater during serpentine formation should result in low-B fluids with δ11B values higher than that of seawater. We utilize a mechanistic B isotope fractionation model coupled with a Bayesian inversion approach to robustly evaluate fluid-rock interactions and propose that Lost City fluid B compositions reflect a two-step process. Initially, B is progressively removed from seawater during serpentine formation, resulting in the generation of an effectively B-free serpentinizing fluid. Subsequently, B remobilized from previously formed serpentine is transferred to later fluids, resulting in the low-δ11B end-member values analyzed. Such an evolution demonstrates that fluid-rock interactions both add fluid-mobile elements such as B to mantle peridotites as well as remobilize them. Thus, analyzed serpentine δ11B signatures reflect the cumulative impact of B-consuming serpentinization and later B-remobilizing serpentinite recrystallization. Consequently, serpentinite alteration, in addition to serpentinization hydration reactions, actively contributes to chemical exchange between the mantle and the oceans. Published: 2026-07-10T21:19:01+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.574 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Aled D. Evans; Heather J. Goring-Harford; Rosalind M. Coggon; Rachael H. James; Caitlin McCarthy; Matthew J. Cooper; Gavin L. Foster; Gretchen L. Früh-Green; Susan Q. Lang; Damon A.H. Teagle

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54880.1

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

Hydrothermal vent fluids reflect interactions between oceanic lithosphere and seawater, but to date, the processes resulting in unusual hyperalkaline (up to pH 11) fluids venting ultramafic-hosted systems, such as the Lost City Hydrothermal Field, remain enigmatic. To elucidate fluid reaction pathways, we present boron (B) concentration and isotope analyses from fluids venting the Lost City, located off axis atop the serpentinized Atlantis Massif just west of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge at 30°N. Our data reveal that fluid B concentrations are markedly lower and isotopically lighter than that of seawater. These data contrast with the accepted understanding of B isotope systematics that predicts that the progressive removal of B from seawater during serpentine formation should result in low-B fluids with δ11B values higher than that of seawater. We utilize a mechanistic B isotope fractionation model coupled with a Bayesian inversion approach to robustly evaluate fluid-rock interactions and propose that Lost City fluid B compositions reflect a two-step process. Initially, B is progressively removed from seawater during serpentine formation, resulting in the generation of an effectively B-free serpentinizing fluid. Subsequently, B remobilized from previously formed serpentine is transferred to later fluids, resulting in the low-δ11B end-member values analyzed. Such an evolution demonstrates that fluid-rock interactions both add fluid-mobile elements such as B to mantle peridotites as well as remobilize them. Thus, analyzed serpentine δ11B signatures reflect the cumulative impact of B-consuming serpentinization and later B-remobilizing serpentinite recrystallization. Consequently, serpentinite alteration, in addition to serpentinization hydration reactions, actively contributes to chemical exchange between the mantle and the oceans.

item.title: Structurally Controlled Deposition of Gold During Inversion of Sedimentary Basin: A Case Study of Sediment-Hosted Orogenic Gold in the Gaolong Deposit, Youjiang Basin, South China Block
item.dc:creator: Yushan Zeng
Lin Yang
Qingfei Wang
Jun Deng
Artur P. Deditius
Jian-Xin Zhao
Ting Cheng
Shiyu Zhao
Shicai Huang
Di Ruan
item.description: Abstract The Youjiang basin underwent late Paleozoic expansion and Triassic inversion, resulting in scattered Paleozoic isolated carbonate platforms surrounded by Triassic turbidites. The Gaolong gold deposit is representative of sediment-hosted orogenic gold deposits formed along isolated platforms in the central-southern basin, where three stages of deformation were identified. The pre-ore D1 deformation in Gaolong features a WNW-trending brachy-anticline and NNE-trending dextral strike-slip shearing within the competent carbonate platform, overthrust of the turbidites onto the carbonate platform along the lithological contacts, and WNW-trending thrusts and related asymmetric tight folds in incompetent turbidites. Kinematic analyses revealed that northeast-southwest to north northeast–south southwest regional shortening occurred. The U-Pb dating of the synkinematic calcite constrains the D1 deformation age of 240.5 ± 5.2 Ma. Syn-ore D2 deformation is associated with north-south shortening and features NE-striking sinistral and NW-striking dextral strike-slip mineralized faults that cut through the D1 structures. Gold orebodies formed in the stratigraphic-structural traps, like tight folds, thrusts, and strike-slip faults in turbidites at the less compressed and permeable eastern and western edges of the platform. Gold mineralization in those edges is mainly invisible gold in the rims of zoned pyrite and arsenopyrite in quartz veins, hydraulic breccia, and altered turbidites with ore-related silicification, sulfidation, sericitization, and minor carbonatization. The inferred stress field and published bitumen Re-Os age suggest that the D1 and D2 deformations were formed during Triassic inversion and record a change in direction of the propagation process of basin inversion. The post-ore D3-1 deformation is characterized by west-northwest–east-southeast shortening, whereas the latest post-ore D3-2 deformation represents extension. The north-northeastward basin inversion progressively propagated from the southernmost toward the northernmost of the Youjiang basin during the Middle to Late Triassic. This inversion involved horizontal crustal shortening and vertical thickening, leading to overpressure of deep fluids and triggering large-scale vertical fluid migration via regional inverted preexisting faults. These faults served as conduits, linking the deep source with shallower traps in the fold-and-thrust belt. At shallow depths, gold deposition during compression is controlled by lithological and rheological contacts along competent isolated platforms, which provide better permeability and Fe-bearing reactants that facilitate the precipitation of gold-bearing sulfide. The geochronology of progressive basin evolution and regional sediment-hosted gold mineralization reveals a coupled spatiotemporal relationship between them. Both the sedimentary facies and structural architecture preparation during basin formation and the following structural inversion and lithological-rheological conditions during basin inversion jointly controlled gold deposition. The identified processes for the formation of sediment-hosted orogenic gold deposits can be used to guide mineral exploration in inverted sedimentary basins worldwide. Published: 2026-07-08T19:37:57+00:00 Venue: Economic Geology Score: 0.574 (consider)

Authors: Yushan Zeng; Lin Yang; Qingfei Wang; Jun Deng; Artur P. Deditius; Jian-Xin Zhao; Ting Cheng; Shiyu Zhao; Shicai Huang; Di Ruan

Journal: Economic Geology

DOI: 10.5382/econgeo.5236

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

Abstract The Youjiang basin underwent late Paleozoic expansion and Triassic inversion, resulting in scattered Paleozoic isolated carbonate platforms surrounded by Triassic turbidites. The Gaolong gold deposit is representative of sediment-hosted orogenic gold deposits formed along isolated platforms in the central-southern basin, where three stages of deformation were identified. The pre-ore D1 deformation in Gaolong features a WNW-trending brachy-anticline and NNE-trending dextral strike-slip shearing within the competent carbonate platform, overthrust of the turbidites onto the carbonate platform along the lithological contacts, and WNW-trending thrusts and related asymmetric tight folds in incompetent turbidites. Kinematic analyses revealed that northeast-southwest to north northeast–south southwest regional shortening occurred. The U-Pb dating of the synkinematic calcite constrains the D1 deformation age of 240.5 ± 5.2 Ma. Syn-ore D2 deformation is associated with north-south shortening and features NE-striking sinistral and NW-striking dextral strike-slip mineralized faults that cut through the D1 structures. Gold orebodies formed in the stratigraphic-structural traps, like tight folds, thrusts, and strike-slip faults in turbidites at the less compressed and permeable eastern and western edges of the platform. Gold mineralization in those edges is mainly invisible gold in the rims of zoned pyrite and arsenopyrite in quartz veins, hydraulic breccia, and altered turbidites with ore-related silicification, sulfidation, sericitization, and minor carbonatization. The inferred stress field and published bitumen Re-Os age suggest that the D1 and D2 deformations were formed during Triassic inversion and record a change in direction of the propagation process of basin inversion. The post-ore D3-1 deformation is characterized by west-northwest–east-southeast shortening, whereas the latest post-ore D3-2 deformation represents extension. The north-northeastward basin inversion progressively propagated from the southernmost toward the northernmost of the Youjiang basin during the Middle to Late Triassic. This inversion involved horizontal crustal shortening and vertical thickening, leading to overpressure of deep fluids and triggering large-scale vertical fluid migration via regional inverted preexisting faults. These faults served as conduits, linking the deep source with shallower traps in the fold-and-thrust belt. At shallow depths, gold deposition during compression is controlled by lithological and rheological contacts along competent isolated platforms, which provide better permeability and Fe-bearing reactants that facilitate the precipitation of gold-bearing sulfide. The geochronology of progressive basin evolution and regional sediment-hosted gold mineralization reveals a coupled spatiotemporal relationship between them. Both the sedimentary facies and structural architecture preparation during basin formation and the following structural inversion and lithological-rheological conditions during basin inversion jointly controlled gold deposition. The identified processes for the formation of sediment-hosted orogenic gold deposits can be used to guide mineral exploration in inverted sedimentary basins worldwide.

item.title: Neoproterozoic lamprophyre from the Southern Granulite Terrane, India: Implications for calc-alkaline magmatism in an aborted rift setting
item.dc:creator: M.R. Nandu
Y. Anilkumar
M.P. Manu Prasanth
Cheng-Xue Yang
M. Santosh
Sanghoon Kwon
Sung Won Kim
A.P. Pradeepkumar
Yoshiyuki Iizuka
S. Anbazhagan
Tamilarasan Kuppusamy
item.description: Calc-alkaline lamprophyres, owing to their distinctive geochemical signature, serve as crucial probes for deciphering continental-scale geodynamic processes as well as the tectonomagmatic evolution of the continental lithosphere. Here we present petrological, mineral chemical, whole-rock geochemical, and U-Pb zircon geochronological data on the calc-alkaline lamprophyre (spessartite) from the Salem Mafic–Ultramafic Complex (SMUC) of the Southern Granulite Terrane (SGT), India. The Salem lamprophyre exhibits a characteristic porphyritic to panidiomorphic texture dominated by amphibole (magnesio-hornblende) phenocrysts. Clinopyroxene (diopside) occurs as microphenocrysts, while feldspar (albite) is largely confined to the groundmass. The high Mg# values (80–83) and elevated concentrations of compatible trace elements (Cr and Ni) indicate the primitive nature of the melt, whereas the incompatible trace element ratios exclude any significant crustal contamination and closely resemble those of subduction-related calc-alkaline lamprophyres reported globally. Elevated Ba/Nb and low Nb/La ratios indicate derivation from an enriched lithospheric mantle source. The pronounced negative Ti–Nb–Ta and Zr-Hf anomalies, together with diagnostic geochemical ratios (e.g., Hf/Sm, Ta/La, Nb/Yb, La/Nb, Ba/Nb), suggest that the mantle source was metasomatized predominantly by dehydrated fluids released from a subducting slab. Zircon grains from the Salem lamprophyre exhibit well-developed magmatic crystallization features, and LA–ICP–MS U-Pb analyses yield a weighted mean age of 805 ± 2.7 Ma (MSWD = 0.54), which is comparable with the Tonian (ca. 830–720 Ma) alkaline–carbonatite magmatism in the region. We envisage that the Neoarchean subduction-modified lithospheric mantle was reactivated and partially melted during Neoproterozoic extension to generate the hydrous parental magma of the Salem calc-alkaline lamprophyre. Published: 2026-07-10T15:36:16+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.569 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: M.R. Nandu; Y. Anilkumar; M.P. Manu Prasanth; Cheng-Xue Yang; M. Santosh; Sanghoon Kwon; Sung Won Kim; A.P. Pradeepkumar; Yoshiyuki Iizuka; S. Anbazhagan; Tamilarasan Kuppusamy

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108710

Relevance: 0.569 (consider)

Calc-alkaline lamprophyres, owing to their distinctive geochemical signature, serve as crucial probes for deciphering continental-scale geodynamic processes as well as the tectonomagmatic evolution of the continental lithosphere. Here we present petrological, mineral chemical, whole-rock geochemical, and U-Pb zircon geochronological data on the calc-alkaline lamprophyre (spessartite) from the Salem Mafic–Ultramafic Complex (SMUC) of the Southern Granulite Terrane (SGT), India. The Salem lamprophyre exhibits a characteristic porphyritic to panidiomorphic texture dominated by amphibole (magnesio-hornblende) phenocrysts. Clinopyroxene (diopside) occurs as microphenocrysts, while feldspar (albite) is largely confined to the groundmass. The high Mg# values (80–83) and elevated concentrations of compatible trace elements (Cr and Ni) indicate the primitive nature of the melt, whereas the incompatible trace element ratios exclude any significant crustal contamination and closely resemble those of subduction-related calc-alkaline lamprophyres reported globally. Elevated Ba/Nb and low Nb/La ratios indicate derivation from an enriched lithospheric mantle source. The pronounced negative Ti–Nb–Ta and Zr-Hf anomalies, together with diagnostic geochemical ratios (e.g., Hf/Sm, Ta/La, Nb/Yb, La/Nb, Ba/Nb), suggest that the mantle source was metasomatized predominantly by dehydrated fluids released from a subducting slab. Zircon grains from the Salem lamprophyre exhibit well-developed magmatic crystallization features, and LA–ICP–MS U-Pb analyses yield a weighted mean age of 805 ± 2.7 Ma (MSWD = 0.54), which is comparable with the Tonian (ca. 830–720 Ma) alkaline–carbonatite magmatism in the region. We envisage that the Neoarchean subduction-modified lithospheric mantle was reactivated and partially melted during Neoproterozoic extension to generate the hydrous parental magma of the Salem calc-alkaline lamprophyre.

item.title: Calcium Solubility of Bridgmanite in Subducted Basalt in Earth's Lower Mantle
item.dc:creator: Chengwei Zhang
Takashi Yoshino
Elias El Ghazaoui
Stella Chariton
Vitali B. Prakapenka
Luo Li
Yanyao Zhang
Jung‐Fu Lin
item.description: Abstract The calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the resulting abundance of davemaoite under lower‐mantle pressure‐temperature conditions have been under debate following a recent report of extensive calcium dissolution in bridgmanite. We experimentally investigated the calcium solubility in bridgmanite in a basaltic composition at pressures up to 125 GPa and temperatures up to 3,520 K. Our experimental data are modeled thermodynamically along the bridgmanite‐davemaoite solvus to quantify the calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the davemaoite proportion in basaltic and pyrolitic assemblages along lower mantle geotherms. Our results indicate that bridgmanite hosts only 0.001–0.03 Ca cations per formula unit in the lower mantle, while davemaoite remains as an abundant mineral, accounting for 8 and 25 vol% of pyrolitic and basaltic mineralogical models, respectively, along representative lower‐mantle geotherms. Our thermoelastic modeling further indicates that the effect of calcium dissolution on the density and bulk sound velocity of lower‐mantle assemblages is negligible. Published: 2026-07-10T13:21:22+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.569 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chengwei Zhang; Takashi Yoshino; Elias El Ghazaoui; Stella Chariton; Vitali B. Prakapenka; Luo Li; Yanyao Zhang; Jung‐Fu Lin

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2026gl121981

Relevance: 0.569 (consider)

Abstract The calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the resulting abundance of davemaoite under lower‐mantle pressure‐temperature conditions have been under debate following a recent report of extensive calcium dissolution in bridgmanite. We experimentally investigated the calcium solubility in bridgmanite in a basaltic composition at pressures up to 125 GPa and temperatures up to 3,520 K. Our experimental data are modeled thermodynamically along the bridgmanite‐davemaoite solvus to quantify the calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the davemaoite proportion in basaltic and pyrolitic assemblages along lower mantle geotherms. Our results indicate that bridgmanite hosts only 0.001–0.03 Ca cations per formula unit in the lower mantle, while davemaoite remains as an abundant mineral, accounting for 8 and 25 vol% of pyrolitic and basaltic mineralogical models, respectively, along representative lower‐mantle geotherms. Our thermoelastic modeling further indicates that the effect of calcium dissolution on the density and bulk sound velocity of lower‐mantle assemblages is negligible.

item.title: Petrogenesis of mafic microgranular enclaves in the Lingshan granitic intrusion, eastern South China: new insights from X-ray CT analysis and macro elemental mapping
item.dc:creator: Yun-Hao Sun
Kong-Yang Zhu
Lin-Lin She
Feng-Qi Zhang
Yi-Xian Xu
Chuan-Wan Dong
Zhong-Yue Shen
item.description: This study investigates mafic microgranular enclaves (MMEs) within the Lingshan granite (Jiangxi Province, South China) through an integrated approach combining traditional geochemistry, macro-X-ray fluorescence (MA-XRF) mapping, and X-ray computed tomography (X-CT). Zircon U-Pb ages and Hf isotopes indicate that the MMEs share similar crystallization ages and isotopic ratio with their host granite. X-CT imaging reveals similar short-axis and long-axis mineral orientations (biotite, hornblende, magnetite, ilmenite and some heavy minerals) between MMEs and host rock, supporting flow alignment of mafic magma globules within the felsic melt (globules of a more mafic magma injected into the felsic magma and subsequently mingled and mixed with it) rather than a restite, or early cumulate origin (the mineral orientations of MMEs in these models are nearly impossible to be consistent with those of the host granite.). Whole-rock geochemistry exhibits non-linear trace element trends, which are inconsistent with simple magma mixing but rather an example of the chaotic nature of the mixing process. MA-XRF mapping further demonstrates enrichment of Fe, Mn, Ti, Y, Nb, and Ca alongside depletion of Sr, K, and Rb in MMEs, attributed to diffusion-driven exchange. The hybridization process weakened the A-type signature of the host granite, implying that some I-type granites may derive from A-type precursors, primarily through magma mixing. Notably, MMEs may sequester some critical ore-forming elements (e.g., Nb, Ta), potentially suppressing the metallogenic potential of the host granite. These findings may provide new constraints on magma differentiation processes and related mineralization mechanisms in South China's granitic systems. Published: 2026-07-09T09:26:53+00:00 Venue: Journal of the Geological Society Score: 0.566 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yun-Hao Sun; Kong-Yang Zhu; Lin-Lin She; Feng-Qi Zhang; Yi-Xian Xu; Chuan-Wan Dong; Zhong-Yue Shen

Journal: Journal of the Geological Society

DOI: 10.1144/jgs2025-093

Relevance: 0.566 (consider)

This study investigates mafic microgranular enclaves (MMEs) within the Lingshan granite (Jiangxi Province, South China) through an integrated approach combining traditional geochemistry, macro-X-ray fluorescence (MA-XRF) mapping, and X-ray computed tomography (X-CT). Zircon U-Pb ages and Hf isotopes indicate that the MMEs share similar crystallization ages and isotopic ratio with their host granite. X-CT imaging reveals similar short-axis and long-axis mineral orientations (biotite, hornblende, magnetite, ilmenite and some heavy minerals) between MMEs and host rock, supporting flow alignment of mafic magma globules within the felsic melt (globules of a more mafic magma injected into the felsic magma and subsequently mingled and mixed with it) rather than a restite, or early cumulate origin (the mineral orientations of MMEs in these models are nearly impossible to be consistent with those of the host granite.). Whole-rock geochemistry exhibits non-linear trace element trends, which are inconsistent with simple magma mixing but rather an example of the chaotic nature of the mixing process. MA-XRF mapping further demonstrates enrichment of Fe, Mn, Ti, Y, Nb, and Ca alongside depletion of Sr, K, and Rb in MMEs, attributed to diffusion-driven exchange. The hybridization process weakened the A-type signature of the host granite, implying that some I-type granites may derive from A-type precursors, primarily through magma mixing. Notably, MMEs may sequester some critical ore-forming elements (e.g., Nb, Ta), potentially suppressing the metallogenic potential of the host granite. These findings may provide new constraints on magma differentiation processes and related mineralization mechanisms in South China's granitic systems.

item.title: Sub-solidus hydrology controls the formation of high-grade tin deposits in granites
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Krattiger
Jonas Köpping
Philipp Weis
Thomas Driesner
item.description: Tin deposits form primarily in and above the roof of granitic intrusions, commonly with important resources of other strategic and critical raw materials (e.g., W and Li). The metals are extracted by magmatic fluids exsolved from the crystallising intrusion; however, it is unclear how the fluids are collected and focused to form spatially confined high-grade ore bodies. Here, we present numerical simulations showing that: (1) most tin-bearing fluids that exsolve from a granitic intrusion during cooling and crystallisation are not expelled but can remain stored in the crystallising intrusion, and (2) efficient fluid mobilisation, focusing, and ore precipitation occur as naturally emerging sub-solidus processes. Hydrothermal convection in the overlying country rock – driven by the magmatic heat – cools the intrusion, which enhances permeability and induces a lateral flow of mobilised magmatic fluids towards strongly localised zones of intense magmatic-meteoric fluid mixing, an efficient mechanism for tin precipitation. Temperatures, pressures, compositions, and time scales of the simulated fluid evolution closely match field-based observations. We find that different tin precipitation mechanisms proposed in the literature ‒ fluid mixing, cooling, and boiling ‒ may all be reconciled as variations of this hydrological process in response to varying geologic conditions such as intrusion depth and/or minor variations in permeability. Our simulations show that magmatic fluids can remain stored post-crystallisation until near the ductile-to-brittle transition, thereby introducing a novel process for fluid focusing that may facilitate magmatic-hydrothermal ore formation and providing a natural interpretation for sub-solidus granite alteration. Published: 2026-07-10T10:21:51+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.565 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Nicolas Krattiger; Jonas Köpping; Philipp Weis; Thomas Driesner

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120186

Relevance: 0.565 (consider)

Tin deposits form primarily in and above the roof of granitic intrusions, commonly with important resources of other strategic and critical raw materials (e.g., W and Li). The metals are extracted by magmatic fluids exsolved from the crystallising intrusion; however, it is unclear how the fluids are collected and focused to form spatially confined high-grade ore bodies. Here, we present numerical simulations showing that: (1) most tin-bearing fluids that exsolve from a granitic intrusion during cooling and crystallisation are not expelled but can remain stored in the crystallising intrusion, and (2) efficient fluid mobilisation, focusing, and ore precipitation occur as naturally emerging sub-solidus processes. Hydrothermal convection in the overlying country rock – driven by the magmatic heat – cools the intrusion, which enhances permeability and induces a lateral flow of mobilised magmatic fluids towards strongly localised zones of intense magmatic-meteoric fluid mixing, an efficient mechanism for tin precipitation. Temperatures, pressures, compositions, and time scales of the simulated fluid evolution closely match field-based observations. We find that different tin precipitation mechanisms proposed in the literature ‒ fluid mixing, cooling, and boiling ‒ may all be reconciled as variations of this hydrological process in response to varying geologic conditions such as intrusion depth and/or minor variations in permeability. Our simulations show that magmatic fluids can remain stored post-crystallisation until near the ductile-to-brittle transition, thereby introducing a novel process for fluid focusing that may facilitate magmatic-hydrothermal ore formation and providing a natural interpretation for sub-solidus granite alteration.

item.title: Selective melt extraction of an enriched lithospheric boundary explains rejuvenated OIB
item.dc:creator: Angus Rogers
Oliver Nebel
Hugh O’Neill
Yona Jacobsen
Xueying Wang
Mark A. Kendrick
Fabio Capitanio
Gregory M. Yaxley
item.description: Rejuvenated volcanism on ocean islands represents a resurgence of volcanic activity that postdates mantle plume magmatism, however the mantle source and melting conditions of the generated lavas remain elusive. Aitutaki Island in the central Pacific is an exceptional case of rejuvenated volcanism ∼8 Myr after shield-stage volcanism. Here we present new olivine data, radiogenic isotope and major-trace element data that show a previously undocumented geochemical bimodality in the island’s lavas. Major-trace element data support low-degree melt extraction from the base of the lithosphere, typical of alkalic OIB. Our mineral data and trace element and isotopic modelling indicate an enriched pyroxenitic source fluxed by carbonatitic melt at 3 GPa best reproduces the rejuvenated lavas. This depth of melting corresponds to the breakdown of pargasitic amphibole, and the appearance of a slow seismic velocity anomaly. Our investigation of the rejuvenated lavas from Aitutaki suggests this boundary layer contains enriched pockets of partial melt amongst depleted restite from plume volcanism. Hafnium-Nd systematics imply carbonatite may be a common component of this boundary layer. We argue that under hotter thermal regimes the volatile-depleted restite is more effectively sampled, leading to greater isotopic similarities between shield and rejuvenated lavas than under cooler regimes. Selective sampling of stored components also explains the isotopic kinship between separate islands along a hotspot trace, including at Aitutaki, Rarotonga and Samoa. These results support the presence of a long-lived, enriched partial-melt boundary layer at the base of the lithosphere and show how its reactivation can generate rejuvenated volcanism millions of years after volcanic extinction. Published: 2026-07-09T10:33:25+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.565 (consider)

Authors: Angus Rogers; Oliver Nebel; Hugh O’Neill; Yona Jacobsen; Xueying Wang; Mark A. Kendrick; Fabio Capitanio; Gregory M. Yaxley

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120221

Relevance: 0.565 (consider)

Rejuvenated volcanism on ocean islands represents a resurgence of volcanic activity that postdates mantle plume magmatism, however the mantle source and melting conditions of the generated lavas remain elusive. Aitutaki Island in the central Pacific is an exceptional case of rejuvenated volcanism ∼8 Myr after shield-stage volcanism. Here we present new olivine data, radiogenic isotope and major-trace element data that show a previously undocumented geochemical bimodality in the island’s lavas. Major-trace element data support low-degree melt extraction from the base of the lithosphere, typical of alkalic OIB. Our mineral data and trace element and isotopic modelling indicate an enriched pyroxenitic source fluxed by carbonatitic melt at 3 GPa best reproduces the rejuvenated lavas. This depth of melting corresponds to the breakdown of pargasitic amphibole, and the appearance of a slow seismic velocity anomaly. Our investigation of the rejuvenated lavas from Aitutaki suggests this boundary layer contains enriched pockets of partial melt amongst depleted restite from plume volcanism. Hafnium-Nd systematics imply carbonatite may be a common component of this boundary layer. We argue that under hotter thermal regimes the volatile-depleted restite is more effectively sampled, leading to greater isotopic similarities between shield and rejuvenated lavas than under cooler regimes. Selective sampling of stored components also explains the isotopic kinship between separate islands along a hotspot trace, including at Aitutaki, Rarotonga and Samoa. These results support the presence of a long-lived, enriched partial-melt boundary layer at the base of the lithosphere and show how its reactivation can generate rejuvenated volcanism millions of years after volcanic extinction.

item.title: Mobilization of insoluble Fe(III) and phosphate in sulfate- and carbonate-rich fluids
item.dc:creator: Huan Chen
Richen Zhong
Yuling Xie
Xinfu Zhao
Joël Brugger
Hao Cui
Chang Yu
Mojie Li
item.description: Iron(III) oxides and phosphates are considered to be insoluble in geofluids but are common products of hydrothermal mineralization. We investigated the capability of sulfate- and/or carbonate-rich fluids to dissolve hematite and apatite using a hydrothermal diamond anvil cell (HDAC). We found that substantial Fe(III) can be dissolved in these fluids, challenging the view that hydrothermal Fe transport requires the predominance of Fe(II). This provides insights into the genetic link between sulfate-rich lithologies (e.g., evaporites and carbonatites) and Fe mineralization in iron oxide-apatite (IOA), iron oxide copper-gold (IOCG), and carbonatite-related deposits. In addition, the co-mobility of phosphate and Fe(III) in these fluids not only explains their coexistence in a series of deposit types, but also implies that some conventionally immobile components (e.g., high field strength elements) may be mobile in sulfate- or carbonate-rich “unconventional” fluids. Published: 2026-07-09T20:08:57+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.564 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Huan Chen; Richen Zhong; Yuling Xie; Xinfu Zhao; Joël Brugger; Hao Cui; Chang Yu; Mojie Li

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54368.1

Relevance: 0.564 (consider)

Iron(III) oxides and phosphates are considered to be insoluble in geofluids but are common products of hydrothermal mineralization. We investigated the capability of sulfate- and/or carbonate-rich fluids to dissolve hematite and apatite using a hydrothermal diamond anvil cell (HDAC). We found that substantial Fe(III) can be dissolved in these fluids, challenging the view that hydrothermal Fe transport requires the predominance of Fe(II). This provides insights into the genetic link between sulfate-rich lithologies (e.g., evaporites and carbonatites) and Fe mineralization in iron oxide-apatite (IOA), iron oxide copper-gold (IOCG), and carbonatite-related deposits. In addition, the co-mobility of phosphate and Fe(III) in these fluids not only explains their coexistence in a series of deposit types, but also implies that some conventionally immobile components (e.g., high field strength elements) may be mobile in sulfate- or carbonate-rich “unconventional” fluids.

item.title: Sulphur, oxygen, and carbon isotope geochemistry of the Windy Craggy mafic-siliciclastic (Besshi-type) volcanogenic massive sulphide deposit, British Columbia
item.dc:creator: Amr Metwally
Jan M. Peter
Evelyne Leduc
Matthew I. Leybourne
item.description: The Windy Craggy deposit in northwestern British Columbia is the world’s largest mafic siliciclastic (Besshi-type) volcanogenic massive sulfide (VMS) deposit, containing ~300 Mt grading 1.38 wt.% Cu, 0.069 wt.% Co, and 0.2 g/t Au. To evaluate the sources of sulfur and carbon, and assess magmatic contributions to ore formation, we analysed sulfur, carbon, and oxygen isotopes in massive sulphide, carbonate gangue minerals, and host rocks. Whole-rock sulfur isotope compositions range from δ34S = −10.1 to +4.8‰ and are consistent with mixing between sulfur leached from footwall volcanic rocks and magmatic sulfur modified by SO2 disproportionation, with only a minor possible sedimentary contribution. Carbonate δ13C values range from −11.8 to +29.6‰ (mostly −5.9 to +7.7‰), whereas δ18O values range from +7.0 to +26.6‰. Most samples define a mixing trend between mantle-derived carbon and marine carbonate, indicating variable inputs of magmatic CO2 and seawater-derived carbon. Trace elements commonly enriched in magmatic fluids (As, Ag, Mo, Sb, and Tl) are strongly correlated and increase with decreasing δ34S. Principal component analysis similarly links these elements with δ13C and total carbon, providing compelling evidence that magmatic volatiles supplied sulfur, carbon, and metals to the Windy Craggy hydrothermal system. Published: 2026-07-11T12:07:46+00:00 Venue: International Geology Review Score: 0.563 (consider)

Authors: Amr Metwally; Jan M. Peter; Evelyne Leduc; Matthew I. Leybourne

Journal: International Geology Review

DOI: 10.1080/00206814.2026.2699156

Relevance: 0.563 (consider)

The Windy Craggy deposit in northwestern British Columbia is the world’s largest mafic siliciclastic (Besshi-type) volcanogenic massive sulfide (VMS) deposit, containing ~300 Mt grading 1.38 wt.% Cu, 0.069 wt.% Co, and 0.2 g/t Au. To evaluate the sources of sulfur and carbon, and assess magmatic contributions to ore formation, we analysed sulfur, carbon, and oxygen isotopes in massive sulphide, carbonate gangue minerals, and host rocks. Whole-rock sulfur isotope compositions range from δ34S = −10.1 to +4.8‰ and are consistent with mixing between sulfur leached from footwall volcanic rocks and magmatic sulfur modified by SO2 disproportionation, with only a minor possible sedimentary contribution. Carbonate δ13C values range from −11.8 to +29.6‰ (mostly −5.9 to +7.7‰), whereas δ18O values range from +7.0 to +26.6‰. Most samples define a mixing trend between mantle-derived carbon and marine carbonate, indicating variable inputs of magmatic CO2 and seawater-derived carbon. Trace elements commonly enriched in magmatic fluids (As, Ag, Mo, Sb, and Tl) are strongly correlated and increase with decreasing δ34S. Principal component analysis similarly links these elements with δ13C and total carbon, providing compelling evidence that magmatic volatiles supplied sulfur, carbon, and metals to the Windy Craggy hydrothermal system.

item.title: Zircon geochronology, geochemistry and bulk-rock Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic characteristics of Neoarchean metamorphic volcanics in the Yinshan Block and tectonic implications
item.dc:creator: Xiaoguang Liu
Yixun Chen
Chen Zhao
Xiao Wang
item.description: The nature of Neoarchean tectonism in the Yinshan Block of the North China Craton (NCC) remains a subject of debate, with competing models proposing either subduction-driven horizontal tectonics or mantle plume-dominated vertical tectonics. This ambiguity largely stems from poorly constrained timing and genetic links between magmatic and metamorphic events. In this study, we present new zircon U-Pb geochronology and Lu-Hf isotopes, whole-rock geochemistry, and Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic data for metamorphosed volcanic rocks of the Xinghe Group and granitic gneisses from the central Yinshan Block. Our results establish a refined chronological framework revealing three distinct magmatic pulses at ca. 2650 Ma, 2550 Ma, and 2520 Ma, followed by a regional granulite-facies metamorphic event between 2520 Ma and 2500 Ma. Geochemical and isotopic analyses indicate a temporal shift in magma sources. The early Neoarchean (ca. 2650 Ma) volcanic rocks display depleted mantle signatures with low initial 87Sr/86Sr, positive εNd(t), and evident zircon Hf positive values, consistent with derivation from a juvenile mantle source with limited crustal addition. Meanwhile, the geochemical signatures of the 2.8–2.7 Ga zircons reveal a source related to mid-ocean ridge (MOR) or ocean island (OI) settings and intermediate crystallization pressure. In contrast, the late Neoarchean (ca. 2520 Ma) granitoids exhibit variable isotopic compositions and zircon negative zircon εHf(t) excursions, indicative of significant crustal recycling and a mixed source. Integrating these findings, we propose a two-stage geodynamic evolution for the Yinshan Block: an early stage dominated by mantle-derived magmatism (all pre-2.6 Ga magmatic records) and a late stage characterized by intensive crustal thickening and enhanced crust-mantle interaction (2.52–2.50 Ga). This evolutionary pattern mirrors observations in other cratons globally, providing key insights into the geodynamic evolution of the early Earth. Published: 2026-07-09T20:11:55+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.562 (consider)
