The nature of Neoarchean tectonism in the Yinshan Block of the North China Craton (NCC) remains a subject of debate, with competing models proposing either subduction-driven horizontal tectonics or mantle plume-dominated vertical tectonics. This ambiguity largely stems from poorly constrained timing and genetic links between magmatic and metamorphic events. In this study, we present new zircon U-Pb geochronology and Lu-Hf isotopes, whole-rock geochemistry, and Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic data for metamorphosed volcanic rocks of the Xinghe Group and granitic gneisses from the central Yinshan Block. Our results establish a refined chronological framework revealing three distinct magmatic pulses at ca. 2650 Ma, 2550 Ma, and 2520 Ma, followed by a regional granulite-facies metamorphic event between 2520 Ma and 2500 Ma. Geochemical and isotopic analyses indicate a temporal shift in magma sources. The early Neoarchean (ca. 2650 Ma) volcanic rocks display depleted mantle signatures with low initial 87Sr/86Sr, positive εNd(t), and evident zircon Hf positive values, consistent with derivation from a juvenile mantle source with limited crustal addition. Meanwhile, the geochemical signatures of the 2.8–2.7 Ga zircons reveal a source related to mid-ocean ridge (MOR) or ocean island (OI) settings and intermediate crystallization pressure. In contrast, the late Neoarchean (ca. 2520 Ma) granitoids exhibit variable isotopic compositions and zircon negative zircon εHf(t) excursions, indicative of significant crustal recycling and a mixed source. Integrating these findings, we propose a two-stage geodynamic evolution for the Yinshan Block: an early stage dominated by mantle-derived magmatism (all pre-2.6 Ga magmatic records) and a late stage characterized by intensive crustal thickening and enhanced crust-mantle interaction (2.52–2.50 Ga). This evolutionary pattern mirrors observations in other cratons globally, providing key insights into the geodynamic evolution of the early Earth.

item.title: Tracing the Origin and Hydrothermal Overprint of Magnetite-Apatite Mineralization in the New Afton Cu-Au ± PGE Alkalic Porphyry Deposit: Insights from Magnetite Textures and Chemistry
item.dc:creator: Andres Felipe Gonzalez-Duran
Adam C. Simon
Robert Holder
John F.H. Thompson
Devin Wade
Catherine Ryan
Ryan Mathur
item.description: Abstract Alkalic porphyry deposits are significant sources of Cu-Au ± platinum group elements (PGE), with magnetite being particularly abundant in the Triassic alkalic systems of British Columbia, Canada. The New Afton area hosts both Cu-Au ± PGE mineralization and several massive magnetite lodes, which were historically mined for iron. The origin of these magnetite orebodies and their relationship to alkalic porphyry mineralization remains unclear. This study integrates field observations and magnetite chemistry and textures to elucidate their formation. We identify two stages of magnetite mineralization: an early high-temperature (>500°C) magmatic-hydrothermal stage, characterized by ilmenite exsolution lamellae, high-Ti magnetite, and symplectitic intergrowths with ilmenite, followed by a later stage of hydrothermal reequilibration that precipitated ilmenite-free Ti-depleted magnetite. Rather than preserving its original crystallization signature, magnetite records a complex history of hydrothermal activity, including Cu-Au-PGE-bearing magmatic-hydrothermal fluid circulation and repeated, superimposed coupled dissolution-reprecipitation events. These findings align with those from other magmatic-hydrothermal ore systems such as iron oxide copper-gold and Kiruna-type iron oxide-apatite deposits, emphasizing the widespread nature of magnetite reequilibration and the need for caution when using magnetite as a petrogenetic indicator. Published: 2026-07-08T19:38:19+00:00 Venue: Economic Geology Score: 0.561 (consider)

Relevance: 0.561 (consider)

Abstract Alkalic porphyry deposits are significant sources of Cu-Au ± platinum group elements (PGE), with magnetite being particularly abundant in the Triassic alkalic systems of British Columbia, Canada. The New Afton area hosts both Cu-Au ± PGE mineralization and several massive magnetite lodes, which were historically mined for iron. The origin of these magnetite orebodies and their relationship to alkalic porphyry mineralization remains unclear. This study integrates field observations and magnetite chemistry and textures to elucidate their formation. We identify two stages of magnetite mineralization: an early high-temperature (>500°C) magmatic-hydrothermal stage, characterized by ilmenite exsolution lamellae, high-Ti magnetite, and symplectitic intergrowths with ilmenite, followed by a later stage of hydrothermal reequilibration that precipitated ilmenite-free Ti-depleted magnetite. Rather than preserving its original crystallization signature, magnetite records a complex history of hydrothermal activity, including Cu-Au-PGE-bearing magmatic-hydrothermal fluid circulation and repeated, superimposed coupled dissolution-reprecipitation events. These findings align with those from other magmatic-hydrothermal ore systems such as iron oxide copper-gold and Kiruna-type iron oxide-apatite deposits, emphasizing the widespread nature of magnetite reequilibration and the need for caution when using magnetite as a petrogenetic indicator.

item.title: Petrological evidence for Neoproterozoic subduction in the Kabul Block, Afghanistan
item.dc:creator: Shah Wali Faryad
Hezbullah Moiny
Hamidullah Waizy
item.description: The Kabul Block in central Afghanistan exposes Neoarchean to Neoproterozoic basement units that record two distinct metamorphic events: a Paleoproterozoic granulite-facies metamorphism and a Neoproterozoic pressure-dominated amphibolite-facies metamorphism. The granulite-facies basement units that experienced an amphibolite-facies overprint are tectonically overlain by younger amphibolite-facies units. Here we present the first evidence for Neoproterozoic blueschist-facies metamorphism in the Kabul Block, documented by mineral textures in amphibolite-facies micaschists from the upper units. These textures record a complex metamorphic evolution involving an earlier high-pressure/ low-temperature event followed by a Neoproterozoic Barrovian-type metamorphic overprint. Thermodynamic calculations and diffusion modelling on two garnet generations were applied to these rocks to constrain metamorphic P-T evolution of these two events and duration of the Barrovian metamorphism. The results, in combination with available age data, indicate that this metamorphism was related to the Neoproterozoic crustal thickening that occurred subsequent to subduction and was coeval with the Grenvillian orogeny. In the Kabul Block, this event took place around 900 Ma and, according to diffusion modelling, lasted for 20–50 Ma, depending on the peak temperature reached (612–677 °C). Published: 2026-07-09T20:50:16+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.561 (consider)

The Kabul Block in central Afghanistan exposes Neoarchean to Neoproterozoic basement units that record two distinct metamorphic events: a Paleoproterozoic granulite-facies metamorphism and a Neoproterozoic pressure-dominated amphibolite-facies metamorphism. The granulite-facies basement units that experienced an amphibolite-facies overprint are tectonically overlain by younger amphibolite-facies units. Here we present the first evidence for Neoproterozoic blueschist-facies metamorphism in the Kabul Block, documented by mineral textures in amphibolite-facies micaschists from the upper units. These textures record a complex metamorphic evolution involving an earlier high-pressure/ low-temperature event followed by a Neoproterozoic Barrovian-type metamorphic overprint. Thermodynamic calculations and diffusion modelling on two garnet generations were applied to these rocks to constrain metamorphic P-T evolution of these two events and duration of the Barrovian metamorphism. The results, in combination with available age data, indicate that this metamorphism was related to the Neoproterozoic crustal thickening that occurred subsequent to subduction and was coeval with the Grenvillian orogeny. In the Kabul Block, this event took place around 900 Ma and, according to diffusion modelling, lasted for 20–50 Ma, depending on the peak temperature reached (612–677 °C).

item.title: Ocean acidification as a planetary signal linking Earth system memory to deep-time lithosphere–ocean geochemical interactions
item.dc:creator: Sumanta Das
Malini Roy Choudhury
item.description: Ocean acidification (OA) is widely recognized as a defining chemical signature of the Anthropocene ocean, yet it is still predominantly framed as a near-surface ecological stressor. Here, we advance a planetary-scale reinterpretation of OA as an information-bearing signal that links rapid anthropogenic carbon forcing to deep-time lithosphere–ocean geochemical interactions and long-lived Earth system memory. Observations since the late 20th century document a persistent, spatially heterogeneous decline in global surface-ocean pH, corresponding to a ∼30–35% increase in hydrogen ion concentration, driven by sustained air–sea CO2 uptake. Projected pH declines of 0.3–0.4 units by 2100 under high-emission scenarios imply rates of change at least an order of magnitude faster than most natural variations recorded in the geological archive. We synthesize carbonate system thermodynamics, carbonate compensation depth dynamics, sedimentary proxy evidence, and Earth system modeling to demonstrate how small, rapid surface perturbations propagate vertically, trigger widespread carbonate undersaturation, and imprint durable stratigraphic and geochemical signatures through sediment dissolution, altered burial fluxes, and delayed alkalinity restoration. Comparison with hyperthermal events, particularly the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum, reveals that the uniqueness of modern OA lies not in magnitude alone but in an unprecedented rate mismatch that overwhelms geological buffering mechanisms. Furthermore, we identify critical knowledge gaps regarding irreversibility, sedimentary signal emergence, and threshold behavior under rapid forcing. Overall, this study reframes OA as a planetary-scale diagnostic of whole-Earth system disequilibrium, revealing its fundamental significance for evaluating long-term carbon cycle stability, Earth system resilience, and the enduring geological legacy of anthropogenic emissions beyond human timescales. Published: 2026-07-10T23:18:16+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.559 (consider)

Ocean acidification (OA) is widely recognized as a defining chemical signature of the Anthropocene ocean, yet it is still predominantly framed as a near-surface ecological stressor. Here, we advance a planetary-scale reinterpretation of OA as an information-bearing signal that links rapid anthropogenic carbon forcing to deep-time lithosphere–ocean geochemical interactions and long-lived Earth system memory. Observations since the late 20th century document a persistent, spatially heterogeneous decline in global surface-ocean pH, corresponding to a ∼30–35% increase in hydrogen ion concentration, driven by sustained air–sea CO2 uptake. Projected pH declines of 0.3–0.4 units by 2100 under high-emission scenarios imply rates of change at least an order of magnitude faster than most natural variations recorded in the geological archive. We synthesize carbonate system thermodynamics, carbonate compensation depth dynamics, sedimentary proxy evidence, and Earth system modeling to demonstrate how small, rapid surface perturbations propagate vertically, trigger widespread carbonate undersaturation, and imprint durable stratigraphic and geochemical signatures through sediment dissolution, altered burial fluxes, and delayed alkalinity restoration. Comparison with hyperthermal events, particularly the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum, reveals that the uniqueness of modern OA lies not in magnitude alone but in an unprecedented rate mismatch that overwhelms geological buffering mechanisms. Furthermore, we identify critical knowledge gaps regarding irreversibility, sedimentary signal emergence, and threshold behavior under rapid forcing. Overall, this study reframes OA as a planetary-scale diagnostic of whole-Earth system disequilibrium, revealing its fundamental significance for evaluating long-term carbon cycle stability, Earth system resilience, and the enduring geological legacy of anthropogenic emissions beyond human timescales.

item.title: The Carbonatitic Origin and Paragenesis of Rare Earth Element Mineralization in the Unusual Taipingzhen Deposit, Central China
item.dc:creator: Wei Zhang
Wei Terry Chen
Anthony E. Williams-Jones
item.description: Abstract The Taipingzhen deposit is characterized by a rarely documented assemblage of rare earth element (REE) minerals. In addition to bastnäsite, parisite, monazite, synchysite, and allanite, which are the main REE-bearing minerals in many magmatic-hydrothermal REE deposits, it contains a large proportion of uncommon REE minerals, including fluocerite, taipingite, fluorbritholite, törnebohmite, and ferrigatelite, the genesis of which is poorly understood. The Taipingzhen orebodies are spatially and temporally associated with carbonatite dikes, and the ores have C and O isotope compositions similar to those of the carbonatites, indicating that the mineralization and carbonatites are genetically related. The order of formation of the REE minerals was controlled by the evolution of ore-forming fluids. Fluocerite was the earliest REE mineral and precipitated in response to an increase in pH that accompanied fluid phase separation. The subsequent precipitation of taipingite and fluorbritholite was triggered by further increases in pH due to the interaction of ore fluids with wall rocks. Törnebohmite and ferrigatelite began crystallizing in response to the increase in Al activity of the hydrothermal fluids that accompanied ongoing fluid-rock interaction. Bastnäsite and parisite precipitated as the fluids cooled. Monazite and allanite coprecipitated with bastnäsite and parisite due to the introduction of P, Al, and Fe as a result of fluid-rock interaction. The early formed REE minerals (e.g., fluocerite, taipingite, and fluorbritholite) were replaced extensively in the late hydrothermal stages by bastnäsite and parisite. Our study documents the relative instability of the early REE minerals, thereby explaining why they rarely survive to record the full mineralizing history of magmatic-hydrothermal REE systems elsewhere. Published: 2026-07-08T19:38:28+00:00 Venue: Economic Geology Score: 0.558 (consider)

Abstract The Taipingzhen deposit is characterized by a rarely documented assemblage of rare earth element (REE) minerals. In addition to bastnäsite, parisite, monazite, synchysite, and allanite, which are the main REE-bearing minerals in many magmatic-hydrothermal REE deposits, it contains a large proportion of uncommon REE minerals, including fluocerite, taipingite, fluorbritholite, törnebohmite, and ferrigatelite, the genesis of which is poorly understood. The Taipingzhen orebodies are spatially and temporally associated with carbonatite dikes, and the ores have C and O isotope compositions similar to those of the carbonatites, indicating that the mineralization and carbonatites are genetically related. The order of formation of the REE minerals was controlled by the evolution of ore-forming fluids. Fluocerite was the earliest REE mineral and precipitated in response to an increase in pH that accompanied fluid phase separation. The subsequent precipitation of taipingite and fluorbritholite was triggered by further increases in pH due to the interaction of ore fluids with wall rocks. Törnebohmite and ferrigatelite began crystallizing in response to the increase in Al activity of the hydrothermal fluids that accompanied ongoing fluid-rock interaction. Bastnäsite and parisite precipitated as the fluids cooled. Monazite and allanite coprecipitated with bastnäsite and parisite due to the introduction of P, Al, and Fe as a result of fluid-rock interaction. The early formed REE minerals (e.g., fluocerite, taipingite, and fluorbritholite) were replaced extensively in the late hydrothermal stages by bastnäsite and parisite. Our study documents the relative instability of the early REE minerals, thereby explaining why they rarely survive to record the full mineralizing history of magmatic-hydrothermal REE systems elsewhere.

item.title: Early diagenetic dolomitization in methane seep carbonates constrained by coupled Mo and Mg isotopes
item.dc:creator: Meng Jin
Zice Jia
Jie Li
Xun Sun
Shaomin Lin
Jörn Peckmann
Qinxian Wang
Duofu Chen
Dong Feng
item.description: Marine carbonates are widely regarded as excellent archives for recording the geochemical evolution of seawater. However, the long-standing “dolomite problem” has hindered the reconstruction of formation mechanisms and depositional settings of the widespread occurrences of dolomite throughout Earth’s history. Cold seeps, where sulfate-driven anaerobic oxidation of methane (SD-AOM) promotes the formation of dolomite, are key to deciphering low-temperature dolomitization processes. Here, we investigate late Miocene to early Pleistocene seep dolomite from Chiahsien, Taiwan, together with modern seep dolomite from the Gulf of Mexico and South China Sea, using a coupled molybdenum (Mo) and magnesium (Mg) isotope approach to constrain the dolomitization environment and processes within seep systems. The wide range of authigenic Mo isotope compositions (δ98Moauth: 0.29 to 2.94‰), correlating with Fe/Al ratios, tracks changes of methane flux spanning from diffusion-dominated (enrichment of heavy Mo) to oxide-shuttle-dominated (enrichment of light Mo) regimes. This contrasts with the homogeneous δ26Mg values of seep dolomite (−2.72 ± 0.21‰), reflecting equilibration with seawater during dolomitization in a shallow, porewater environment with high replenishment of seawater Mg. The Chiahsien seep dolomite further displays higher crystallographic ordering expressed as I(015)/I(110), generally lower Sr/(Mg + Ca) ratios, and more euhedral crystals than modern seep dolomite. Taken together, our new observations suggest that precursor carbonate minerals (aragonite, calcite) were dolomitized during early diagenesis at shallow depth. The lack of Rayleigh fractionation effects of Mg isotope distinguishes seep dolomite from dolomite formed in more restricted, deeper settings, such as environments dominated by organic sulfate reduction and methanogenesis. This study demonstrates that combined Mo–Mg isotope systematics can effectively decipher the redox history and the process of dolomitization. Furthermore, it suggests that seep dolomite, which tends to form in open, seawater-buffered porewater environments, is a promising archive for reconstructing the Mg isotope composition of ancient seawater. Published: 2026-07-13T05:46:57+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.556 (consider)

Marine carbonates are widely regarded as excellent archives for recording the geochemical evolution of seawater. However, the long-standing “dolomite problem” has hindered the reconstruction of formation mechanisms and depositional settings of the widespread occurrences of dolomite throughout Earth’s history. Cold seeps, where sulfate-driven anaerobic oxidation of methane (SD-AOM) promotes the formation of dolomite, are key to deciphering low-temperature dolomitization processes. Here, we investigate late Miocene to early Pleistocene seep dolomite from Chiahsien, Taiwan, together with modern seep dolomite from the Gulf of Mexico and South China Sea, using a coupled molybdenum (Mo) and magnesium (Mg) isotope approach to constrain the dolomitization environment and processes within seep systems. The wide range of authigenic Mo isotope compositions (δ98Moauth: 0.29 to 2.94‰), correlating with Fe/Al ratios, tracks changes of methane flux spanning from diffusion-dominated (enrichment of heavy Mo) to oxide-shuttle-dominated (enrichment of light Mo) regimes. This contrasts with the homogeneous δ26Mg values of seep dolomite (−2.72 ± 0.21‰), reflecting equilibration with seawater during dolomitization in a shallow, porewater environment with high replenishment of seawater Mg. The Chiahsien seep dolomite further displays higher crystallographic ordering expressed as I(015)/I(110), generally lower Sr/(Mg + Ca) ratios, and more euhedral crystals than modern seep dolomite. Taken together, our new observations suggest that precursor carbonate minerals (aragonite, calcite) were dolomitized during early diagenesis at shallow depth. The lack of Rayleigh fractionation effects of Mg isotope distinguishes seep dolomite from dolomite formed in more restricted, deeper settings, such as environments dominated by organic sulfate reduction and methanogenesis. This study demonstrates that combined Mo–Mg isotope systematics can effectively decipher the redox history and the process of dolomitization. Furthermore, it suggests that seep dolomite, which tends to form in open, seawater-buffered porewater environments, is a promising archive for reconstructing the Mg isotope composition of ancient seawater.
